items.heading: Is automated collector upgrade available?
items.text: Currently, we do not provide an automated process to upgrade on-premises data collectors. With the new release of the WXP Collaboration Admin Portal, we do have a way to identify a weekly maintenance window, as shown in the screenshot below.

items.heading: What is the Relevance of Maintenance Window?
items.text: Setting up a maintenance window notifies HP product support team that you are allowing them to update part or all of your on-premises data collector during this window on a weekly basis. Here are the scenarios where Product Support would push out an upgrade to your on-premises data collector. However, it's not guaranteed that you will be upgraded. Note: Cloud collectors that support the collection of the following infrastructure are not part of the Collector Maintenance, and could be updated, upgraded, or restarted at any given time. We try to keep the window on these actions between 5–7 PM Pacific Time weeknights.Microsoft TeamsZoom (Meetings and Phone)Webex (including Control Hub)Office 365Google (Meet and Calendar)BlueJeansRingCentral Note: Cloud collectors that support the collection of the following infrastructure are not part of the Collector Maintenance, and could be updated, upgraded, or restarted at any given time. We try to keep the window on these actions between 5–7 PM Pacific Time weeknights.

items.heading: Emergency patches
items.text: In the case of an emergency, we may need to patch your collector to address a security or vulnerability issue that is impacting your collectors' operation. This could also be done outside of the maintenance window. For this type of patch, do ensure your Security team is set up as a Security contact in the Account Settings in the Admin Portal of WXP Collaboration application.

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: The Office365 (O365) connector is a cloud-to-cloud integration that allows WXP Collaboration to retrieve calendar scheduling data from collaboration rooms and combine it with endpoint data. This enables comprehensive visibility and detailed analysis across your meeting spaces. This article explains how to set up the O365 connector.

items.heading: Prerequisite
items.text: Global Administrator Access is required. The authorizing user must have Global Administrator access within the Microsoft Office 365 tenant to authorize WXP Collaboration data collection.

items.heading: Required Scopes
items.text: Microsoft is deprecating the Legacy Exchange Web Services (EWS) connection and has introduced an alternative using the Graph API. The Graph API enables WXP Collaboration to access your O365 tenant using specific permissions called scopes, via an Azure Enterprise Application. After a global admin consents, an application named Office365 GraphAPI Collector is created. Below are the scopes requested by WXP Collaboration during authorization. Scope Permission Microsoft Reference Calendars.Read Allows WXP Collaboration to read calendar events. Link Directory.Read.All Allows WXP Collaboration to read data in your organization's directory, such as users, groups, and apps. Place.Read.All Allows WXP Collaboration to read company places (conference rooms and room lists) for calendar events. User.Read Allows WXP Collaboration to sign-in to the app, and allows the app to read the profile of the signed-in users. It also allows the app to read basic company information of sign-in users. User.Read.All Allows WXP Collaboration to read users profiles.

items.heading: Optional: Room Distribution List in O365
items.text: WXP Collaboration Workspace Insights uses a room distribution list to identify which room resources are queried for scheduled meeting data. Typically, these include only rooms with managed endpoints. For instructions on creating a room distribution list, see: How To: Create a Room Distribution List for Workspace Insights with O365 Distribution Lists or O365 Groups are not supported and will not work.

items.heading: O365 Integration Setup
items.text: To begin collecting O365 data, you must add the O365 Collector to your existing data collector.

items.heading: Microsoft Exchange Integration for Workspace Insights
items.text: If your organization uses TMS Exchange Extension (TMSXE) for integration with Exchange, it is likely that the following configuration steps have already been completed. In most cases, WXP Collaboration can utilize the TMSXE user account and distribution list to automatically ingest the room list and calendar data. If you have TMSXE in place, consult with your WXP Collaboration sales engineer before proceeding with the steps in this section.

items.heading: Exchange Config Step 1 - Create a Service Account for Exchange Web Services (EWS)
items.text: Integration with Microsoft Exchange requires your Exchange Administrator to set up a service account for EWS, used to query scheduled meeting data. You must ensure the asterisk/wildcard is added to the end of the user agent string when adding using the Set-CASMailbox command. Set-CASMailbox -Identity svcVyoptaAnalytics -EwsApplicationAccessPolicy EnforceAllowList -EwsAllowList "VyoptaRooms*". If you are running a hybrid environment with Exchange 2013 or above be sure to create the service account in the appropriate environment.

items.heading: Exchange Config Step 2 - Create a Room Distribution List in Exchange
items.text: WXP Collaboration Workspace Insights utilizes an Exchange distribution list to identify which room resources are queried for scheduled meeting data. Depending on your environment, you may not be able to set up a new room distribution list for use with WXP Collaboration Workspace Insights. For these environments, you can instead provide a CSV file that maps the individual ExchangeResourceID to a specific room name. You will be responsible for updating and maintaining this file over time if any room changes are made or required.

items.heading: Exchange Config Step 3 - Add Exchange to the WXP Collaboration Data Collector
items.text: To start collecting Exchange data, you will need to add the Exchange Collector to your current data collector by performing the following:

items.heading: Add Exchange User Agent String to the Source System (if required)
items.text: If your Exchange/O365 environment has configured an 'EwsApplicationAccessPolicy' to 'EnforceAllowList', then you will need to set a configurable parameter by performing the following:
van.exchg.userAgent=VyoptaRooms;

items.heading: Add Exchange Distribution List
items.text: If you configured an Exchange Distribution List as per above, you will need to manually add it by performing the following:
ExchgRoomsSync.poll.roomListAddress=VyoptaRoomsDL@company.com

items.heading: Prerequisite
items.text: You must have Global Administrator role credentials for authorization purposes. Note: Due to the recent Microsoft API retirements, WXP Collaboration now leverages Microsoft InTune (Microsoft’s official device management platform) and its APIs to provide necessary details required to maintain your Microsoft Teams Rooms(MTR) inventory and accurately report call and meeting utilization for each device. If some of your MTRs are NOT managed within InTune, we recommend an additional secondary integration that leverages Microsoft CQD to complete your MTR catalogue.

items.heading: Scope
items.text: WXP Collaboration utilizes Microsoft Graph APIs provided by Microsoft to gather call data and Teams Room data: Scope Type Permission Microsoft Reference CallRecords.Read.All Application Allows WXP Collaboration to read call records for all calls and online meetings Link DeviceManagementManagedDevices.Read.All Allows WXP Collaboration to read the Intune data for Teams devices. User.Read Delegated Allows WXP Collaboration to sign-in to the app and allows the app to read the profile of the signed-in user. User.Read.All Allows WXP Collaboration to read users profiles. TeamworkDevice.Read.All Deprecated by Microsoft as of Dec 2025 Allows WXP Collaboration to read the management data for Teams devices.

items.heading: Missing Microsoft Teams Data in Tech Insights Monitoring
items.text: If you have our Microsoft Teams enabled for Tech Insights Monitoring, we do not collect from live meetings. Instead, we receive all the call data from the Microsoft Graph API after the meeting has completed. Currently, Microsoft has reported that it could take up to 30 minutes before the CDR record is visible on the Teams dashboard and up to 24 hours before it is available via the API. Once WXP Collaboration has received the CDR record for the call following its ending, we will make the data available within a minute to Tech Insights Monitoring. We can only process data from call records since Microsoft does not make real-time data available via the provided APIs. For more on the data API and Microsoft Teams, you can view the following link: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoftteams/cqd-data-and-reports

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Manually Pulling Up CDR Records from CUCM for Push Customers

items.heading: Manually Pulling Up CDR Records from CUCM for Push Customers
Introduction
items.text: In customer environments that use Cisco Unified Communications Manager (CUCM) with push-based Call Detail Records (CDR) collection, missing records can occur due to network issues, File Transfer Protocol (FTP) errors, or processing delays. This article explains how to manually retrieve processed CDR files from CUCM without changing the collection method to CDRonDemand. It lists what you need—Secure Shell (SSH) access to the collector server, FTP details that match the billing server, and a temporary destination folder—and the steps to list, download, extract, and place the CDR files where your collector can process them. This procedure helps restore complete reporting with minimal disruption.

items.heading: Manual CDR records from CUCM for an Environment that Uses Push-Based CUCM CDR Collection
items.text: If a push-based customer is missing CDR records, users can manually pull the records from CUCM without switching the collection method back to CDRonDemand.

items.heading: Steps
items.text: Connect (example) ssh user@cucm.server List processed CDR files file list activelog cm/cdr_repository/processed Retrieve processed CDR files (recursive, compressed) file get activelog cm/cdr_repository/processed recurs compress Note: You can include a sample screenshot of the "file get" command execution here for reference.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Configuring CUCM Push Based CDR Collection

items.heading: Configuring CUCM Push Based CDR Collection
Introduction
items.text: Using an external FTP or SFTP server, the WXP Collaboration Data Collector acts as a billing processor. This allows Cisco Unified Communications Manager (CUCM) to send its Call Detail Records (CDRs) directly to the collector instead of the collector polling them by using the API.Each CUCM supports up to three billing servers. If all three are already in use, the WXP Collaboration Data Collector can still be configured and can forward received CDR files to another system.Push-based CDR collection requires installation of a third-party SFTP server application on the WXP Collaboration Data Collector server. Several options are available. For example, the open-source FreeFTPd server can be configured as described in Collaboration Monitoring - Installing and Configuring FreeFTPd.

items.heading: Enabling Push-Based CDR Collection
items.text: The following steps describe how to configure push-based CDR collection.

items.heading: Step 1: Adding the Billing Server to CUCM
items.text: To configure the Data Collector as a billing server:

Note: If the password must be changed later, update it first on the external FTP/SFTP server. When updated in CUCM, the system attempts to log in with the new password. If login fails, the new password will not be saved.

items.heading: Step 2: Test CDR Send
items.text: 1. Place a test call between endpoints registered on CUCM.2. End the call and verify that the file is sent to the SFTP directory on the server.If there are errors, check the CUCM logs for more details.

items.heading: Step 3: Disable Collection for CUCM
items.text: Once CDR collection is verified, disable collection for the CUCM Publisher systems being set up for push-based CDR collection:

items.heading: Step 4: Configure WXP Collaboration Application for Push CDRs
items.text: Complete the following configuration in the WXP Collaboration Admin Portal.

items.heading: CDR File Collection (Contact WXP Collaboration Support)
items.text: Contact WXP Collaboration Support and request enabling push-based CDR collection for your organization.This can only be completed only by the WXP Collaboration Support team.
Note: Push-based CDR collection must be enabled for all CUCM Publishers in WXP Collaboration. The billing server must be configured on all Publishers before re-enabling collection.

items.heading: CDR Forwarding
items.text: If all three billing servers were already occupied on any of the CUCM Pubs, then it is necessary to use one of the billing server slots for WXP Collaboration and then have WXP Collaboration forward the collected CDRs to the billing server that was replaced. For this, configure the collector to forward the CDR files onward to this server by expanding the Forward menu and entering the FTP configuration used by the original billing server:

items.heading: Step 5: Re-enable Collection for CUCM
items.text: By default, uploads occur every 10 minutes. The interval can be adjusted if needed.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Cisco Telepresence Management Suite (TMS) Integration with WXP Collaboration

items.heading: Cisco Telepresence Management Suite (TMS) Integration with WXP Collaboration
Introduction
items.text: The Cisco TelePresence Management Suite (TMS) integration with the HP WXP Collaboration application is important because it allows organizations to collect data directly from the TMS SQL database. By setting up a read-only service account and adding a TMS Connector, administrators can monitor system usage, view scheduling information, and analyze performance. This integration helps ensure data accuracy, improves troubleshooting, and supports long-term planning for collaboration environments. The integration requires creating a read-only service account in the TMS SQL database and then adding a TMS Connector in the Admin Portal of WXP Collaboration. Complete the steps below in order.

items.heading: Step 1: Setting up a Service Account for Cisco TMS SQL Server Database
items.text: The service account must be added to the TMS SQL Database with read-only privileges. First, determine whether the TMS SQL Server Database is located on the TMS appliance or on a separate SQL Server. After identifying the location, you need administrator access to the server to add the required service account. You may need help from a SQL Server Administrator to provide access or to add the account manually. If the TMS SQL databases are hosted on a separate SQL Server, consult with your organization’s SQL Server DBA to create the required read-only account. There are two ways to identify where your TMS database is located: The read-only database account must be named vyopta_svc. It must have the db_datareader and public roles, as well as access to the tmsng database. If the SQL database resides on the TMS server (less common), contact Support for help with the TMS Preparation Installer. Note: You must obtain the assigned password and the appropriate TCP/IP port for database access. The default port is 1433, but other ports may be configured.

items.heading: Step 2: Adding a TMS Connector
items.text: Before adding a TMS connector, complete these prerequisites:
Note: We recommend using the hostname rather than the IP address, as IP addresses can change. It is also helpful to use a friendly name that is easy to understand.

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: The Cisco Meeting Server (CMS) integration with the WXP Collaboration application is important for monitoring and analytics. By creating a service account and adding CMS to the WXP Collaboration Data Collector, organizations can collect call detail records (CDRs), track system usage, and view performance data. This information helps IT administrators maintain service quality, troubleshoot issues, and plan for future capacity needs.

items.heading: Integration Steps
items.text: The integration requires two main tasks: creating a service account in Cisco Meeting Server (CMS) and adding the CMS Connector in the Admin Portal of WXP Collaboration application. Follow these steps in sequence to complete the setup.

items.heading: Step 1: Setting up a Service Account for Cisco Meeting Server (CMS)
items.text: To use the CMS meeting and user reporting APIs, you must create a service account with read-only administrator access.

Note: CMS passwords expire after 180 days by default. This means you must update the service account password twice a year. If your organization allows, you can change the password policy before creating the vyopta_svc account.To change the default password expiration period, log into the CMS command line interface with the admin account (but before adding the Vyopta_svc account), and enter this command: user rule password_age NNNN.Replace NNNN with the number of days before a password expires. For example, to set the expiration to 5 years, run: user rule password_age 1825If the vyopta_svc account is already created and you want to extend its expiration, run the command:passwd vyopta_svcConfirm with the administrator password.Re-enter the current password for vyopta_svc.You do not need to change the password. If you do change it, update the password in the WXP Collaboration Apps Management Portal Note: CMS passwords expire after 180 days by default. This means you must update the service account password twice a year. If your organization allows, you can change the password policy before creating the vyopta_svc account. To change the default password expiration period, log into the CMS command line interface with the admin account (but before adding the Vyopta_svc account), and enter this command: user rule password_age NNNN.Replace NNNN with the number of days before a password expires. For example, to set the expiration to 5 years, run: user rule password_age 1825 If the vyopta_svc account is already created and you want to extend its expiration, run the command:passwd vyopta_svc

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: CMS Capacity Calculation in WXP Collaboration

items.heading: CMS Capacity Calculation in WXP Collaboration
Introduction
items.text: When calculating CMS capacity metrics in WXP Collaboration, the following input values are used:

items.heading: System Capacity
items.text: System capacity is calculated based on the configured server type, which can be X2, X3, or VM. The value is expressed as the Media Tokens Limit. WXP Collaboration uses Media Token values to calculate concurrent HD port utilization.

items.heading: Current HD Port Usage
items.text: Current usage is calculated from data transmitted by the bridge. Video data token usage is determined by video stream characteristics such as picture width, height, and frame rate. For license consumption, usage is grouped into bands for CMS:

items.heading: How WXP Collaboration Receives the Data
items.text: The system type and media tokens key data are stored in the Analytics platform.They are also transmitted by the Data Collector in the real-time data stream.Real-time data also includes the current HD port usage.

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: The Cisco Unified Border Element (CUBE) integration with the WXP Collaboration application is important because it enables the collection of call quality and performance data directly from Cisco gateways. By configuring SNMP, SSH, and media statistics, and then adding CUBE to the WXP Collaboration Admin Portal, administrators can monitor call detail, track dial-peer status, and measure metrics such as jitter, packet loss, and latency. This integration helps ensure visibility into voice performance, improves troubleshooting, and supports proactive management of collaboration services. This article describes the steps and configuration commands needed to set up Cisco Unified Border Element (CUBE) for data collection and to add CUBE to the WXP Collaboration.

items.heading: CUBE Configuration
items.text: To prepare CUBE for integration with WXP Collaboration, you must configure SSH access, SNMP settings, dial-peer monitoring, and media statistics.

items.heading: Step 1: Installing SSH Client
items.text: Before starting, install an SSH client on the server that will host the WXP Collaboration Collector.

items.heading: Step 2: Opening Ports
items.text: Open all required ports between the server hosting the collector and the CUBE(s). This may require opening ports on a firewall or allowing traffic through access control lists in the network.

items.heading: Step 3: Configure ISR/CUBE for SNMP access
items.text: Example configuration for SNMP v2: snmp-server community vyoptacpm ROsnmp-server enable traps dialsnmp-server host <data collector host IP address> version 2c vyoptacpm Example configuration for SNMP v3: SNMP version 3 was introduced in version 4.8.7 of our Data Collector, should you need SNMPv3 support, please upgrade the collector to the latest version

items.heading: Step 4: Enable SSH
items.text: Enable SSH on port 22. Create a limited IOS service account for WXP Collaboration Tech Insights. A different privilege level can be used at your discretion. The levels below are the minimum required. privilege configure all level 5 loggingprivilege exec all level 5 show dial-peer voice summaryprivilege exec all level 5 show dial-peer voiceprivilege exec level 5 show dial-peerprivilege exec level 5 show Create the required service account with SSH access to ISR/CUBE: username vyopta_svc privilege 5 secret <password>

items.heading: Step 5 – Enable status tracking on individual dial-peers per router
items.text: For each dial-peer, configure Out-of-dialog (OOD) OPTIONS Ping for specified SIP. dial-peer voice tag voip voice-class sip options-keepalive {up-interval seconds | down-interval seconds | retry retries} Example: enableconfigure terminaldial-peer voice 200 voipvoice-class sip options-keepalive up-interval 100 down-interval 50 retry 6 The above values are the tested and recommended values.

items.heading: Step 6: Enable media statistics
items.text: Enable global tracking of jitter, packet loss, and latency. voice service voip media statistics enableconfigure terminalvoice service voipmedia statisticsexit Reference: https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/ios-xml/ios/voice/cube/configuration/cube-book/voi-cube-voice-quality-monitoring.html

items.heading: Adding Cisco Unified Border Element (CUBE) in WXP Collaboration
Introduction
items.text: Cisco Expressway (Core/Edge) and Video Communication Server (VCS) are entry points for video and unified communications. It is used to support mobile and remote access, business-to-business and business-to-consumer calls, and firewall traversal. WXP Collaboration application’s “Data Collector” is a monitoring and analytics tool that allows administrators to gather data from video infrastructure devices. To report availability, capacity, and call quality in, the Data Collector must connect securely to each Expressway or VCS node. This article explains how to create a minimum-privilege, read-only account on Expressway or VCS. It also explains how to add each device in the Admin Portal of WXP Collaboration application.

items.text: To ensure a successful integration, confirm the following before you begin:

items.text: To enable monitoring in WXP Collaboration, follow the below steps. Once these steps are completed, WXP Collaboration application can collect statistics and alerts, such as registrations, call volumes, traversal health, and license usage. This data is useful to troubleshoot quickly and plan capacity with confidence.

items.text: Note: VCS Active Directory (AD) accounts are not supported. The service account must be a local administrator.

items.heading: Step 2: Adding Expressway or VCS in WXP Collaboration Application

items.text: Note: If you have multiple Expressway or VCS-C/E devices in a cluster, add each device (node) individually—for example, primary and secondary peers. Do not add the logical, clustered “named environment.”

items.text: After you create a local, read-only service account and add the device in WXP Collaboration, the Data Collector can monitor Cisco Expressway or VCS for analytics and reporting.

items.heading: Cisco Microsoft Teams Room(MTR) FAQs
items.text: Are Cisco devices in MTR mode supported in WXP Collaboration? Yes! Since Cisco launched native MTR functionality in select hardware devices in 2023, those endpoints have been supported in WXP Collaboration (then Vyopta). Are organizations currently using Cisco MTRs in WXP Collaboration? Yes. Several thousand Cisco MTRs worldwide are actively being monitored in WXP Collaboration. This is one of the fastest growing device segments as many organizations are using MS Teams as their primary meeting platform. Are there restrictions on which Cisco models are supported? No. Any Cisco model that supports MTR mode will be supported in WXP Collaboration. After migrating my Cisco device to MTR mode, what changes in functionality should I expect? In general, the device will perform exactly as expected, but there are a couple of important things to note. My Cisco endpoints are being monitored locally for WXP Collaboration, will that still work after switching to MTR mode? Yes! The local endpoint monitor will still work and provide the same level of functionality for up/down events, peripheral status, and the like. The only difference is, as described above, real time in-call status changes won’t be available. My Cisco endpoints are providing real time occupancy and people-count data for Space Insights, will that continue to work after switching to MTR mode? Yes! Your device will provide the same level of data for real time occupancy and people counting metrics. Are new endpoint models/modes tested for support in WXP Collaboration? Definitely. Since joining forces with HP, our hands-on testing capabilities have become much more robust as we share the same (well-equipped) lab environment with HP Poly. MTR mode was tested thoroughly on multiple Cisco models prior to rolling out support. * Future roadmap support is planned to identify device “sameness” across diverging unique identifiers and multiple vendors to maintain a single device record in WXP Collaboration.

items.heading: Zoom Phone Device Location in Tech Insights Analytics
Introduction
items.text: Accurate device location is important for reporting, inventory alignment, site-level analytics, and operations such as troubleshooting and chargeback. In Zoom Phone, several location sources are available. For example, user, phone number, and device. This article describes the location that the WXP Collaboration application uses from the Zoom Phone device to set the location in the Managed Endpoint dataset.

items.heading: Location Sources in Zoom Phone
items.text: Zoom Phone master data provides three location sources, each with a Site ID and Site Name:

items.heading: Determining Device Location
items.text: For the Managed Endpoint dataset, WXP Collaboration uses the Device Site (Site ID and Site Name) associated with the Zoom Phone device. This value most accurately represents the endpoint’s assigned location and aligns consistently with device inventory and analytics views.

items.heading: Zoom Meetings Integration
items.text: With the new Zoom Connector, you can connect your Zoom account using OAuth, with optional support for Zoom QSS. Zoom QSS is a Zoom offering that allows WXP Collaboration to collect quality metrics for all your Zoom meetings. Before enabling Zoom QSS, contact your Zoom Account Manager to confirm if QSS is enabled on your account.

items.heading: Add Zoom to WXP Collaboration
items.text: 1. Go to the WXP Collaboration Admin Portal. 2. In the Infrastructure menu, click New Infrastructure (near the bottom). 3. In the list of infrastructure types, click Zoom.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Explaining Pexip Media Load Calculation and Gateway Call Inclusion

items.heading: Explaining Pexip Media Load Calculation and Gateway Call Inclusion
items.text: How is Pexip media load calculated, and does it include gateway calls? The PEXIP API provides WXP Collaboration with the data for media load, which is then displayed at the Management node level. WXP Collaboration does not perform any calculations on this data and presents it as supplied from the PEXIP system. This media load data includes gateway calls. However, it is important to note that the media load calculation methodology is proprietary to PEXIP. If you require further information on how PEXIP calculates media load, it is recommended that you contact PEXIP support directly. Additional details on PEXIP capacity planning can be found in the following link: https://docs.pexip.com/admin/capacity_planning.htm.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Authenticating to use the Query Service API

items.heading: Authenticating to use the Query Service API
items.text: The credentials used to authenticate against the Assurance API are the same as your normal WXP Collaboration user credentials.

items.heading: Obtaining Authorization:
items.text: The API authentication is performed via OpenID, with the credentials being sent as an application/x-www-form-url-encoded payload to a POST request to https://login.wxpcollaboration.hp.com/auth/login. The Content-Type header must be set, and a header with the key of Host must also be set - this header takes a value of login.wxpcollaboration.hp.com.
The encoded payload contains the following key-value pairs:

Upon successful authentication, a 200 response will be received with a JSON payload with the following schema: { "access_token": string, "expires_in": int, "refresh_expires_in": int, "refresh_token": string, "token_type": string, "not_before_policy: long, "session_state": string, "scope": string } Field Description access_token The token (beginning "eyJh") that can be used to access the API expires_in The period of time, in seconds, for which this token is valid (300) refresh_expires_in The period of valid time for the refresh_token. NOT IMPLEMENTED refresh_token A token to refresh the authentication. NOT IMPLEMENTED token_type The authentication token type. Will have the value of "bearer" not_before_policy The start of signing key validity for the access_token session_state Session information scope The basis upon which the token was authenticated. Will have the value of "email profile" An example response (abbreviated) can be seen here: { "access_token":"eyJhb ... -Pdl31W5g", "expires_in":300, "refresh_expires_in":1800, "refresh_token":"ey J...-H5oAwI8", "token_type":"bearer", "not-before-policy":1533920635, "session_state":"dad914da-2d68-4202-a98a-146da4033e1e", "scope":"emailprofile" }

items.heading: Using the Access Token:
items.text: To use the token, add an HTTP header with key Authorization and a value comprised of the word Bearer, a single space, and then the token.
It is worth noting that it is the developer's responsibility to ensure that the token is within its period of validity.

items.heading: Identifying Most and Least Used Conference Rooms
Introduction
items.text: In most organizations, virtual meeting rooms (VMRs) and cloud meeting rooms (CMRs) are essential for day-to-day collaboration. However, not all rooms are used equally, and unused or overused resources can affect efficiency and costs. Understanding which conference rooms are most and least utilized helps IT and facilities teams to make data-driven decisions about capacity planning, licensing, and user adoption. HP WXP Collaboration application provides Advanced Analytics for clear visibility into these usage patterns. This helps IT administrators to optimize meeting experiences and reduce waste. This article cover the multiple ways to identify the most and least utilized VMRs using Advanced Analytics.

items.heading: Option 1: Using the Adoption Feature
items.text: The Adoption tab of Tech Insights Analytics shows an automatically generated panel called Top VMR Summary. This panel can be configured to display data for Acano, CTPS, MCU, Pexip, and RMS systems.
The resulting table provides a summary like this:

Note: Static VMRs, which were not used during the selected time period will not appear in the list. Percentage change is calculated based on the previous data for an equivalent amount of time. For example, if you are viewing data for one month, the percentage change is based on the previous month.

items.heading: Option 2: Using the Datasets Tab
items.text: You can also use the Datasets tab to identify the most and least utilized conference rooms. In the Datasets tab, meetings are displayed in descending order based on the Total Meeting Count. In a similar way, the other usage related columns, such as Total Minutes, can be used to rank utilization. Tip: Use the Date-Range picker at the top right of any tab in Tech Insights Analytics to adjust the date range for your analysis. You can also use the Share or Forward icon at the top right of the page to schedule a one-time or recurring report. Tip:

items.text: In Tech Insights Analytics, under the Meetings tab, you can click the Meeting name to expand the participants and their quality of experience.

We’ll grab his email from the exposed participants in the previous view and set it equal to the Participant Address. Then we can filter the view to only show the calls with bad quality.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: WXP Collaboration Endpoint Monitoring Deployment Guide

items.heading: WXP Collaboration Endpoint Monitoring Deployment Guide
Introduction
items.text: The WXP Collaboration Endpoint Monitoring service gives additional insight into your organization’s video infrastructure. This guide helps you install Endpoint Monitoring in your environment. Follow the steps in order to complete the installation. Note: The Prerequisite for performing any steps in this guide is the completion of the Infrastructure setup steps described in the Tech Insights Deployment Guide - Infrastructure Setup document.

items.heading: Supported endpoints
items.text: WXP Collaboration Endpoint Monitoring supports almost all current Cisco endpoints and most newer Polycom video endpoints. For best results, install the latest firmware on all devices. For the latest support information, refer to the article Vendor Support Matrix.

items.heading: Endpoint Registration and Selection
items.text: There are two methods for adding endpoints for monitoring. Note: Use the administrator service account you created for the WXP Collaboration Data Collector in the steps below. For any assistance, contact your WXP Collaboration Enablement Specialist. Important: Endpoints deleted in TMS will continue to appear in the WXP Collaboration Application Portal unless you also purge them from TMS.
Note: Do not add endpoints to the Public group. Ensure the Public group contains no endpoints.

items.heading: Creating and Managing a Monitoring Group
items.text: Monitoring Groups let you segment endpoints for various administration purposes. For example, by region or business unit.

Note: This stores credentials for Endpoint Monitoring and does not reset them on your video endpoints or infrastructure. Do not add unsupported legacy endpoints to Monitoring Groups. They may cause connectivity errors.

items.heading: Preparation Notes for the Endpoint Monitoring Server
Server Requirements
items.text: Endpoint Monitoring should be installed on the same server or VM as the WXP Collaboration Data Collector. This stores credentials for Endpoint Monitoring and does not reset them on your video endpoints or infrastructure. If you require a separate server, contact support@wxp.hp.com for details.

items.heading: Supporting Workspace Insights
items.text: The Workspace Insights module depends on Endpoint Monitoring to capture both in-call and out-of-call activity data. Workspace Insights requires Endpoint Monitor version 4.0 or later. Download the latest compatible build from the WXP Collaboration Admin Portal.

items.heading: Deploying Tech Insights Endpoint Monitoring in Your Environment
items.text: After preparing your video environment, deploy using the WXP Collaboration Endpoint Monitor configuration utility. The process includes downloading the software, installing it on the server, signing in to the Application Management Portal with the service account, and starting the service.

Congratulations! You have now successfully installed and activated Tech Insights Endpoint Monitoring within your environment. Now, you can see real-time data for endpoints populate into the Tech Insights Monitoring module.

items.heading: Viewing Endpoint Monitor Status
items.text: After the initial endpoint list loads for the assigned Monitoring Group, you can view status on the Endpoints tab.

items.heading: Loading endpoints
items.text: On startup, Endpoint Monitoring loads all endpoints in the Monitoring Group for the service account. Loading time depends on endpoint count and network performance. Rule of thumb: 100–200 endpoints per hour for initial load. If loading fails for an endpoint, the monitor retries every 10 minutes.

items.heading: Performance Characteristics
items.text: The operating characteristics for Endpoint Monitoring are highly variable – network topology, bandwidth, endpoint location, endpoint model/type, and utilization are all factors that will affect performance. Endpoint Monitoring attempts to connect to all endpoints that are within a specified access group. Any endpoints in remote locations or in locations with spotty network performance may experience disruptions in connection. Although a best effort is made to reconnect to these endpoints, these disruptions may cause loss of data. For best results, we recommend initiating your use of Endpoint Monitoring with endpoints with optimal proximity and connectivity to your WXP Collaboration Data Collector server, and then adding endpoints in an organized, incremental fashion thereafter. The recommended threshold for endpoints is 2,000 for a standard server deployment with dual CPUs, with another 1,000 being supported for each additional CPU.

items.heading: Initial Scale Processing and Network Impact
items.text: If you have questions during deployment, contact your WXP Collaboration Enablement Specialist or support@wxp.hp.com.

items.heading: Overview of Experience Score in WXP Collaboration
Introduction
items.text: HP WXP Collaboration is designed to support a strong user experience with collaboration tools. Its User Experience Score feature helps you understand the quality of the user experience and take data-driven action. The User Experience Score is calculated by a proprietary algorithm that considers multiple factors, such as average call quality metrics, average quality of meetings, number of rejoins, meeting failures, and the weighted volume of issues. The score is dynamic and will evolve as collaboration infrastructure and usage patterns evolve.

The Experience Score is available at the individual level in the users dataset. It is most valuable when viewed at scale. You can create a dashboard panel to visualize the average Experience Score over time, broken down by department or other attributes. You can also create a panel to track the Experience Score for VIP users. While the inputs and algorithm can change over time, the following factors are considered: You can use these insights to identify trends, target remediation, and improve the overall quality of collaboration experiences across your organization.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Calculating Utilization Rate in the Endpoint Dataset

items.heading: Calculating Utilization Rate in the Endpoint Dataset
Introduction
items.text: The Utilization Rate column under the Endpoints tab in HP WXP Collaboration’s Tech Insights Analytics is calculated as the utilization percentage during a user-defined window each week. This rate is based upon the user-set values for number of workdays per week to be considered, and the number of hours per workday. This in turn defines the number of hours that represents 100% utilization per week, and by extrapolation, the number of hours in a month, quarter or year. (When using a 7-day week, of course, you can simply use the hours per day as the basis for 100% utilization.) For example, the most utilized endpoint in the example below was used a total of 3361.6 minutes during the month of October. October is 31 days, which means 100% utilization in this case is 31 days x 8 hours/day = 248 hours, or 14,880 minutes. The utilization rate is then simply 3361.6 minutes / 14,880 minutes, or 22.59%.

The utilization workday is set as 7 days / week 8 hours / day by default. You can alter this according to your preference as shown in the example below. Changing the number of days/week and hours/day will change your utilization rate accordingly.

To provide an alternate example using 5 days a week, 8 hours per day as the basis: This means that 40 hours of utilization per week, or 5.71 hours per day, represents 100% utilization. For the same date range, the month of October, 100% utilization would be 31 days x 5.71 hours/day = 177.14 hours = 10,628.57 minutes, meaning the utilization of the top endpoint would be 31.63% (3361.6 minutes / 10,628.57 minutes) in the above case. Tip: Use the date range picker in the top right of any tab within Tech Insights Analytics to change the dates you want to analyze. https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Yesterday’s Data Not Showing in Tech Insights Analytics

items.heading: Yesterday’s Data Not Showing in Tech Insights Analytics
Introduction
items.text: When data from the previous day is missing in Tech Insights Analytics, it can slow investigations, reporting, and executive updates. This article explains how HP WXP Collaboration application’s nightly processing schedule, time-zone settings, and maintenance windows affect when your data appears. It also outlines expected timelines (for example, 24–48 hours for new endpoints and tags), common causes of delay, and creating a support ticket to support your retirement.

items.heading: Common Causes
items.text: If you do not see data from the previous day in Tech Insights Analytics, the cause is often related to collection and processing timing.

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: This article will walk through creating an Event monitor within WXP Collaboration. To read the difference between Events and Metrics, please click here.

items.heading: Creating An Event
items.text: If your organization is enabled for creating Events, you can click on the Add Monitor button, which opens up a dropdown menu. In the dropdown, you can select "Event Monitor".

Now that we're on the Event Monitor Page, let's go over the "Events" section under the "Set filter conditions". There are six different attributes you can choose from. Three of them are visible when you open a new events page: Origin type is used for the type of system you want to accept events for. Currently, the only option for the Event type is "Endpoints". We will be adding other origin types, like System, in the future. The Event Type is what Event you want to get alerted on. There are over 190 different Events to choose from. "Endpoint Down" and "Endpoint In Call" are WXP Collaboration-generated Events, while the rest of the Events are events that Cisco Endpoints have the capability of sending as a Diagnostic Message. WXP Collaboration does not know the type of messages that Cisco Endpoints can/will send. The Level is the level of the event coming from the source system. For Example, if you choose "Camera Pairing" as an event type. The endpoint can have different messages for the error, and with each error, it can have a different event level. So camera pairing event types can either come in as Critical or Warning, for instance. The Level option in WXP Collaboration allows you to either accept any level and get alerted on that, or you can filter down to get only Critical Level Events from the endpoint itself. Message allows you to filter on what messages you want to get alerted on, based on what the system is sending. It has the normal operators that the WXP Collaboration generally provides. It's recommended that if you do not know what messages are coming in, you do not use this filter until you have determined how you want to filter your events. Category currently holds two values. Endpoint Status and Cisco Diagnostics. This is an added filter to narrow down whether you just want to receive Endpoint Status Events or Cisco Diagnostics. The Origin ID is the identifier for the origin object. Currently, this is related to the endpoint you want to select. However, it is recommended you use the Origin Type (e.g, Endpoints) filter. The second section under "Set filter conditions" allows you to filter what endpoints you would like the monitor to keep monitoring. You have the ability to select certain endpoints, use tags, or any other available field in the menu. It's recommended to use tags; that way, as a user, you do not have to constantly add endpoints.

items.heading: Defining a status trigger
items.text: The Define a status trigger allows you to select a status for your events that is not the same as what the endpoint sends for your emails/webhooks and set the duration of how long the event needs to be available before it is triggered as a notification.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Understanding Monitors vs Events Based Monitoring

items.heading: Understanding Monitors vs Events Based Monitoring
Introduction
items.text: WXP Collaboration meticulously gathers a substantial volume of data points from interconnected systems. With this wealth of data, WXP Collaboration offers two distinct monitoring solutions to its customers. This article elucidates these two monitoring solutions and their respective applications.

items.heading: Metrics Based Monitoring
items.text: Metrics-based monitoring represents the prevailing method with which our customers are most acquainted. This approach entails the ceaseless acquisition and examination of quantitative data emanating from diverse facets of a system. These data points are inherently numerical, affording a profound understanding of a system's or call's performance, health, and behavioral patterns over time. Metrics based monitoring excels in evaluating call quality and the volume of calls directed towards a system. However, it may not always be the optimal choice for ascertaining system availability or peripheral status. This is where Event-Based Monitoring steps in, presenting a realm of new possibilities for alerting, a capability that WXP Collaboration was previously unable to offer.

items.heading: Event Based Monitoring
items.text: In stark contrast, Event Based Monitoring is centered on the occurrence of precise events or incidents within a system. This mode of monitoring proves especially effective in promptly detecting and responding to incidents, errors, and other pivotal events. Unlike metrics-based systems, event-based systems operate on defined rules or patterns to trigger alerts. There are many benefits to Event Based Monitoring. It facilitates the monitoring of system availability, peripheral status (coming soon), and, in the case of Cisco endpoints, diagnostics messages. The latter, in particular, is of notable significance, as Cisco endpoints afford access to a staggering array of 194 distinct event types, each accompanied by various message types. Initially, WXP Collaboration concentrated on Cisco endpoints due to the profusion of event types that can be gathered and leveraged for alerts. Nevertheless, we are steadfastly laboring towards incorporating Events from diverse device manufacturers. Creating an Event Monitor for how to use Event Based Monitoring

items.heading: Investigating Unknown Quality with Bridge Calls
items.text: All bridge participant quality is captured in the Tech Insights Monitoring and later summarized and inserted into Tech Insights Analytics. Factors that may result in unknown call quality that may prevent the collection of these real-time data points are: Due to the reasons enumerated above, 100% capture of call quality scores is not to be expected; a 75-80% capture rate is more typical across WXP Collaboration customers. When analyzing bridge call quality, you can use the Tech Insights Analytics tab to review the data. On this tab, you will find two different panels: Bridge Participant Quality and Bridge Participant Quality Trend. The Bridge Participant Quality Trend provides time groupings that reflect the length of time in the overall view. For example, if you select three months, the data can be presented either by month, week, or day. As you expand the date range, you can get a better understanding of the call quality.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Understanding Why Tech Insights Monitoring Flags an Entire Meeting as “Bad” Due to a Single Bad Channel

items.heading: Understanding Why Tech Insights Monitoring Flags an Entire Meeting as “Bad” Due to a Single Bad Channel
Question
items.text: In Tech Insights Monitoring, why do you show the whole meeting as “Bad” when it is just a single bad channel on a single participant?

items.heading: Answer
items.text: Quality for meetings is displayed based on the “worst-case” quality of the call for each participant in the meeting – in other words, the participant with the worst packet loss and/or jitter values dictates the overall meeting quality displayed. We do this to help WXP Collaboration users quickly identify call quality issues and accelerate the troubleshooting process. There are multiple ways to analyze quality issues more deeply. You can view all individual call legs that are currently experiencing bad quality on the Calls tab in Tech Insights Monitoring, as shown in the screenshot below.

If a call is using VMR/bridge technology, you can view the entire meeting on the Meetings tab in Tech Insights Monitoring. If you click on the meeting name or expand the details, you can view all the participants in that meeting and the quality they are currently experiencing.

User's Rooms Attended The count of rooms scheduled during the reporting period for a particular user or group of users in which any WXP Collaboration monitored activity took place in that room between the start and end of the time that room was scheduled for (making that an attended room). For example, if a user, Jane.Doe@acme.com, booked one room for Meeting A from 1:00 PM - 2:00 PM where an audio call was made in that room from 1:05 PM - 1:35 PM and she booked two rooms for Meeting B from 4:00 PM - 5:00 PM where no activity took place in those rooms, the User's Rooms Attended count for Jane.Doe@acme.com for a reporting period of that day would be 1. User's Rooms Missed The count of rooms scheduled during the reporting period for a particular user or group of users in which no WXP Collaboration monitored activity took place in that room between the start and end of the time that room was scheduled for (making that a missed room). For example, if a user, Jane.Doe@acme.com, booked one room for Meeting A from 1:00 PM - 2:00 PM where an audio call was made in that room from 1:05 PM - 1:35 PM and she booked two rooms for Meeting B from 4:00 PM - 5:00 PM where no activity took place in those rooms, the User's Rooms Missed count for Jane.Doe@acme.com for a reporting period of that day would be 2. For example, an "Exec" tag with "true/false" values for each user would allow you to filter and create dashboards for monitoring call quality of the executive team. User Defined Attribute 07 User custom tag #7. You can define up to 10 custom tag columns for the users dataset, including a title for the column and a custom value for each user. For example, an "Exec" tag with "true/false" values for each user would allow you to filter and create dashboards for monitoring call quality of the executive team. User Defined Attribute 08 User custom tag #8. You can define up to 10 custom tag columns for the users dataset, including a title for the column and a custom value for each user. For example, an "Exec" tag with "true/false" values for each user would allow you to filter and create dashboards for monitoring call quality of the executive team. User Defined Attribute 09 User custom tag #9. You can define up to 10 custom tag columns for the users dataset, including a title for the column and a custom value for each user. For example, an "Exec" tag with "true/false" values for each user would allow you to filter and create dashboards for monitoring call quality of the executive team. User Defined Attribute 10 User custom tag #10. You can define up to 10 custom tag columns for the users dataset, including a title for the column and a custom value for each user. For example, an "Exec" tag with "true/false" values for each user would allow you to filter and create dashboards for monitoring call quality of the executive team. DID Assigned Whether this user has a direct inward dial (DID) number assigned to them Voice systems Requires mapping of users to DID numbers DID The direct inward dial (DID) number for this user, if available PSTN Inbound Intended Call Count The number of inbound PSTN calls to this user during the time period For Cisco voice infrastructure, it's required to map at the trunk level. For Zoom phone or MS Teams Phone systems, this is determined automatically. PSTN Outbound Call Count The number of outbound PSTN calls from this user during the time period PSTN Inbound Intended Call Minute The number of inbound PSTN call minutes to this user during the time period PSTN Outbound Call Minute The number of outbound PSTN call minutes from this user during the time period On-Net Call Count Number of calls by this user that were facilitated by trunks flagged as "on-net" CUBE and CUCM If the trunk that carried the call is flagged as "on-net" or "off-net", that will appear here for this call record. Applicable only to CUBE and CUCM. It will be unknown for other systems of if the the on-net / off-net flag is not known for the trunk. On-Net Call Minute Number of minutes in calls by this user that were facilitated by trunks flagged as "on-net" Off-Net Call Count Number of calls by this user that were facilitated by trunks flagged as "off-net" Off-Net Call Minute Number of minutes in calls by this user that were facilitated by trunks flagged as "off-net" Webex Meetings Hosted Count The number of Webex meetings hosted by this user during the time period Cisco Webex Zoom Meetings Hosted Count The number of Zoom meetings hosted by this user during the time period Zoom MS Teams Meetings Hosted Count The number of MS Teams meetings hosted by this user during the time period MS Teams Webex Meetings Hosted Minutes The duration of all Webex meetings hosted by this user during the time period, in minutes Zoom Meetings Hosted Minutes The duration of all Zoom meetings hosted by this user during the time period, in minutes MS Teams Meetings Hosted Minutes The duration of all MS Teams meetings hosted by this user during the time period, in minutes Webex Meetings Attended Count The number of Webex meetings this user attended during the time period Zoom Meetings Attended Count The number of Zoom meetings this user attended during the time period MS Teams Meetings Attended Count The number of MS Teams meetings this user attended during the time period Webex Meetings Attended Minutes The amount of time (in minutes) this user spent attending Webex meetings Zoom Meetings Attended Minutes The amount of time (in minutes) this user spent attending Zoom meetings MS Teams Meetings Attended Minutes The amount of time (in minutes) this user spent attending MS Teams meetings Inbound Call Count The number of inbound calls to this user during the time period Outbound Call Count The number of outbound calls from this user during the time period Inbound Call Minute Total call minutes for inbound calls to this user during the time period Outbound Call Minute Total call minutes for outbound calls from this user during the time period Meetings Attended Count The number of meetings attended by this user during the time period Meetings Attended Minutes The amount of time (in minutes) this user spent attending meetings during the time period Average Quality (Users) The average quality of this user's meeting/call connections during the time period. Average quality is calculated by quantifying the quality ratings (bad = 1, fair = 2, good = 3) of each call and averaging them over the time period Average Meetings Per Week The average number of meetings this user attends per week. Normalized to a weekly cadence for convenience, it's calculated by the total number of meetings divided by the number of weeks in the time period. Average Meeting Hours Per Week The average time spent (hours) in meetings each week for this user. Normalized to a weekly cadence for convenience, it's calculated by the total hours spent in meetings divided by the number of weeks in the time period. Average User Experience Score The average User Experience Score for the time period. The Experience Score is calculated on a monthly cadence. If the time period is greater than one month, this will show the average of the monthly values. If the time period is less than or equal to one month, this will show the Experience Score for that month. To learn more about Experience Score, visit WXP Collaboration's knowledge base. This column was added in Q3 2023. Prior historical records will be empty. CUCM User Is this user known to the organization's CUCM infrastructure? CUCM Requires your organization's CUCM infrastructure to be registered with WXP Collaboration Zoom User Is this user a registered Zoom user? Note: this doesn't infer licensing status in Zoom, only that the person is a registered user. Requires your organization's Zoom instance to be registered with WXP Collaboration Bluejeans User Is this user a registered Bluejeans user? Note: this doesn't infer licensing status in Bluejeans, only that the person is a registered user. Bluejeans Requires your organization's Bluejeans instance to be registered with WXP Collaboration Skype User Is this user a registered Skype user? Note: this doesn't infer licensing status in Skype, only that the person is a registered user. Skype Deprecated Acano User Is this user a registered Acano user? Note: this doesn't infer licensing status in Acano, just being a known user. Acano Exchange User Is this user a registered Exchange user? Exchange Requires your organization's Exchange instance to be registered with WXP Collaboration MS Teams User Is this user a registered MS Teams user? Note: this doesn't infer licensing status in MS Teams, only that the person is a registered user. Requires your organization's MS Teams instance to be registered with WXP Collaboration Google Meet User Is this user a registered Google Meet user? Note: this doesn't infer licensing status in Google Meet, only that the person is a registered user. Google Meet Requires your organization's Google Meet instance to be registered with WXP Collaboration Cisco Webex User Is this user a registered Cisco Webex user? Note: this doesn't infer licensing status in Cisco Webex, only that the person is a registered user. Webex Requires your organization's Cisco Webex instance to be registered with WXP Collaboration Ring Central User Is this user a registered Ring Central user? Note: this doesn't infer licensing status in Ring Central, only that the person is a registered user. RingCentral Requires your organization's Ring Central instance to be registered with WXP Collaboration Zoom Phone User Is this user a registered Zoom Phone user? Note: this doesn't infer Zoom Phone licensing status, only that the person is a registered user. Zoom Phone Requires your organization's Zoom Phone instance to be registered with WXP Collaboration

items.heading: Technology Insights Analytics - Participants
items.text: Column Name Column Description Applicable Systems Comments Participant Address Participant address Display Name Participant's display name Meeting Name Name of the meeting, if available. For certain meeting types where a name is not available (such as P2P, "meet now" options, etc), this will appear as "[host user name]'s Meeting". Note that MS Teams does not provide actual meeting names. Only applicable to meeting platforms such as MS Teams, Webex, Zoom, etc. Not applicable to voice calls. Note that MS Teams meetings will not show the actual meeting name, instead showing a generic name based on the meeting host, such as "[Organizer's] Meeting" Start Time Timestamp of when the participant joined the meeting. End Time Timestamp of when the participant left the meeting (or it ended). Participant Minutes The number of minutes this participant spent in the meeting. Note that this field is for convenience and derived from "Start Time" and "End Time" Avg Source People Count If this participant joined from a group endpoint capable of people counting, this will show the average people count value in that room during the meeting. Only applicable to meeting platforms (not voice calls) where the endpoint in use is capable of people counting. Max Source People Count If this participant joined from a group endpoint capable of people counting, this will show the max people count value in that room during the meeting. Participants (Count) For individual participant connections in this dataset, this number will always be 1. Audio Participants (Count) For individual participant connections in this dataset, this number will be 1 if the participant joined the meeting with audio-only (not video), else 0. Video Participants (Count) For individual participant connections in this dataset, this number will be 1 if the participant joined a video-enabled meeting, else 0. Participant Audio Minutes The number of minutes this participant joined an audio-only meeting Participant Video Minutes The number of minutes this participant joined a video-enabled meeting Protocol The protocol of the pariticipant's connection, determined by the source platform. Call Type (Audio/Video) The modality of this participant's connection (audio or video). Call Direction (Incoming/Outgoing) For voice calls, this will show whether the call was incoming or outgoing. For meeting participants, this will show as outgoing (the participant establishing a connection to the online meeting platform). Server Name For supported platforms, this shows the system location for the participant's connection. Otherwise, it will mirror System Name. Note: currently only supported for Pexip. Quality Quality of this participant's connection (good, fair, bad) as classified by HP WXP Collaboration based on all available factors that impact user experience Disconnect Reason Disconnect reason (request successful, timeout, etc) Connection Status Connection status (connected or disconnected) of the participant's connection. If the value is "Not Connected", the Disconnect Reason column can give further information ("callee busy", "request timeout", etc) Device Pool Name The Device Pool Name as configured in CUCM (relevant for CUCM devices only). CUCM End-of-Life Announcement Date If known, this will show the end-of-life announcement date for the endpoint used, as specified by the manufacturer End-of-New Service Attachment Date If known, this will show the end-of-new-service attachment date for the endpoint used, as specified by the manufacturer End-of-Sale Date If known, this will show the end-of-sale date for the endpoint used, as specified by the manufacturer Endpoint Category Type of endpoint, such as Group, Personal, Software, Phone, Immersive, etc. Endpoint Name Name of the endpoint Endpoint Software Version Software or firmware version of the endpoint used Endpoint Tag Endpoint tag is a custom tag specified by your admin. If the endpoint used has been tagged, it will display here. Endpoint Type (Hardware/Software) Indicates whether this is a hardware or software endpoint (or unknown) Endpoint Type (Internal/Other) Whether this endpoint is a known, internal endpoint Location (Endpoint) This column shows the Location tag that was set for the endpoint used in the call, useful for filtering and aggregating by custom tags. Managed/Unmanaged (Endpoint) Whether the endpoint used is a managed endpoint in WXP Collaboration Manufacturer The manufacturer of the endpoint Model The model of the endpoint. For hardware endpoints, this will show the device model. For software endpoints, this will show the type of software (for instance, "Microsoft Teams for Mac") Serial Number The serial number of the endpoint. Applicable only to hardware endpoints Structured Tag 01 Structured tag for endpoints (1 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. Structured Tag 02 Structured tag for endpoints (2 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. Structured Tag 03 Structured tag for endpoints (3 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. Structured Tag 04 Structured tag for endpoints (4 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. Structured Tag 05 Structured tag for endpoints (5 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. Structured Tag 06 Structured tag for endpoints (6 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. Structured Tag 07 Structured tag for endpoints (7 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. Structured Tag 08 Structured tag for endpoints (8 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. Structured Tag 09 Structured tag for endpoints (9 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. Structured Tag 10 Structured tag for endpoints (10 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. WXP Collaboration Monitored (Endpoint) Whether the endpoint is in a monitoring group in WXP Collaboration Meeting ID The ID of the meeting as provided by the source platform. Meeting Modality (Audio/Video) The modality of the meeting. Regardless of this participant's connection (audio or video), whether the meeting video-enabled, audio-only, or unknown. Meeting Sub Type The sub type of the meeting. Example values are P2P, VMR, and Webinar. Meeting Type (Bridge/P2P) Whether this was a direct P2P meeting or a bridge. Remote Participant Address The remote party that this participant called. For bridge meetings, this will be the meeting name. For P2P meetings, this will be the remote participant. System Name System Name of the platform where the meeting was hosted (such as Teams, Zoom, Webex, etc). System Name is defined by the admin in HP WXP Collaboration when adding a new piece of infrastructure. System Type (Acronym) Acronym of the source system where the meeting was hosted. Example values are ZOOM, MSTEAMS, CISCOWEBEX, P2P, etc. These values are maintained by HP WXP Collaboration. If you want to see the custom name specified by your admin, use System Name. System Type Description Text field describing the system, configured by your administrator (optional) System Unit Deprecated WXP Collaboration Monitored (System) Whether the system that facilitated this meeting is monitored by HP WXP Collaboration City (User) The city of the user associated with this participant, as it appears in the Users dataset. Unknown for guest or external users. Any system where the participant is joining from a logged-in account that maps to a known user in the User dataset. Country (User) The country of the user associated with this participant, as it appears in the Users dataset. Unknown for guest or external users. State (User) The geographic state of the user associated with this participant, as it appears in the Users dataset. Unknown for guest or external users. User Defined Attribute 01 Structured tag for users (1 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. User Defined Attribute 02 Structured tag for users (2 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. User Defined Attribute 03 Structured tag for users (3 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. User Defined Attribute 04 Structured tag for users (4 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. User Defined Attribute 05 Structured tag for users (5 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. User Defined Attribute 06 Structured tag for users (6 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. User Defined Attribute 07 Structured tag for users (7 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. User Defined Attribute 08 Structured tag for users (8 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. User Defined Attribute 09 Structured tag for users (9 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. User Defined Attribute 10 Structured tag for users (10 of 10). This is a freeform tag that can be customized by your administrator. Up to 10 structured tag columns can be configured. User Department The department of the user User E-mail The email address of the user User Domain The domain of the user's email address IP Address (Participant) It's recommended to use "Internal IP Address" or "Public IP Address". This column is for convenience, it will show internal IP address if it is known, but will show public IP address if it is known and internal is unknown. Building Name Name of the building There are two ways this field can be populated. 1) If the endpoint used is mapped to a room in Space Insights, which is in a known building, that building will show here. 2) If network IP subnet mapping has been configured and the IP address of this participant maps to a known building, that building will show here. City (Building) The city where the building is located Based on the attributes of the building, see "Building Name" for more info. Zipcode (Building) The zipcode of the building Country (Building) The country where the building is located State (Building) The state where the building is located Region (Building) The region of the building (user defined). For example, "EMEA", "LATAM", "Midwest", etc. Network Name Name of the internal network this participant was using. If the participant's internal IP address maps to a known network subnet (configured by your administrator), the name of that network will show here. Otherwise it will be unknown. Requires network ip subnet mapping by your administrator Inside Corp If this participant connection is mapped to a known network (see Network Name), this will show the "Inside Corp" flag for that network, which indicates whether the network is part of the organization's internal corporate network. VPN If this participant connection is mapped to a known network (see Network Name), this will show the "VPN" flag for that network, which indicates whether the network is a virtual private network. Quality Feedback If a post-meeting survey was completed (for example, "How was your meeting? 1 star ... 5 stars") the result of that survey will show here. Camera The type of camera used by this participant to enable video. Note that this will be unknown if the user did not enable video, it was an audio-only meeting, the endpoint in use does not report camera type, or for most external participants. Speaker The type of speaker used by this participant to during the meeting, as reported by the participant's endpoint. Microphone The type of microphone used by this participant to during the meeting, as reported by the participant's endpoint. Note that this will be unknown for external participants or if the endpoint does not report microphone type. Network Type The type of network used by this participant. For example, WiFi, wired, mobile, etc. Internal IP Address The internal IP address of the participant's endpoint, as used on the local network. Public IP Address The public IP address of the participant's endpoint, as used to connect to the internet. Platform The OS platform of the endpoint. For example: MacOS, Windows, Linux, iOS, etc. OS Version The OS version number of the endpoint (if known). Average Link Speed (Mbps) The avg link speed in Mbps for the participant's connection to the meeting platform server, as reported by the meeting platform. CPU Load Average Rate The avg CPU load of the participant's device during the meeting, as reported by the meeting platform. Applies to software endpoints. CPU Load Max Rate The max CPU load of the participant's device during the meeting, as reported by the meeting platform. Applies to software endpoints. Media Node The location of the media node (hosted and maintained by the meeting platform) that facilitated this participant's connection to the meeting, as reported by the meeting platform. Device The device type used by this participant. For example: Windows PC, MacbookPro16, Android Device, etc. PSTN Voice This attribute helps to identify if a call was a PSTN or a pure VOIP call. For Cisco a call is tagged PSTN if it had traversed through a PSTN Trunk. For Zoom Phones a call is tagged PSTN based on the Path and Call Type fields. Interop If this participant connection is via a group endpoint that is joining via interop (for instance, a Cisco Webex Room Kit that joins a MS Teams meeting), this field will show as interop. Only applicable to meeting platforms (not voice calls) and group endpoints. Personal or software endpoints are not applicable. Elapsed Join Time (Seconds) Elapsed time for join request (in seconds). This shows the amount of time it took for the participant to actually enter the meeting after clicking to join, including time in "waiting room" Webex, Zoom Microphone Status Whether this participant enabled their microphone during the meeting. A value of "off" indicates the participant stayed muted for the whole meeting. A value of "on" indicates the user went off mute and spoke for part of the meeting. Note that a value of "off" will only appear if the participant had a valid audio receive connection. For instance, if a participant was in a conference room and joined only to present slides (with no audio connection) this would show as "unknown". Camera Status Whether this participant shared content (for instance, sharing their desktop or an app window) during the meeting. Presentation Status Whether this participant enabled their camera during the meeting. In-Office vs Remote Whether this participant joined the meeting from a known internal network or a remote location. This is based on the list of internal network IP subnet ranges. If the internal network have been uploaded and the IP of the participant maps to one of the networks, this field will show "in-office". If it does not map to a known network, it will show "remote". If the list of internal networks have not been uploaded or the IP of the participant is not available, this will show as "unknown". Requires network IP subnet mapping by your administrator
