items.heading: Space Insights Fields
items.text: The following is a list of all Room (Workspaces) Metrics and their definitions in WXP Collaboration. No. Room Metric Description 1 Room Name The name of the room associated with the unique room key (usually the Exchange Resource ID). The source of the Room Name is either pulled directly via the WXP Collaboration Exchange integration or uploaded by the client via CSV. 2 Exchange Resource ID The unique identifier (key) for the room. In scenarios where customers do not have Exchange or O365 and are uploading their calendar data via CSV, they should treat this as their unique room identifier. 3 Endpoint Name (Room) The name of the WXP Collaboration managed endpoint associated with the Exchange Resource ID. 4 Location The location of the "Endpoint Name (Room)" that has been associated with the Exchange Resource ID. 5 Scheduled Meeting Count The number of scheduled meetings for the Exchange Resource ID during the reporting period. 6 Scheduled Meeting Minute The sum of minutes scheduled for the Exchange Resource ID during the reporting period. For example, if the reporting period is one day and an Exchange Resource ID has been scheduled for meetings from 9:00 AM - 10:00 AM and from 2:00 PM - 3:00 PM, the Scheduled Meeting Minute count would be 120. 7 Attended Meeting Count The count of scheduled meetings during the reporting period in which any WXP Collaboration monitored activity took place between the start and end of the scheduled meeting. For example, if a meeting is scheduled in a room (Exchange Resource ID) from 9:00 AM - 10:00 AM and the Endpoint Name (Room) that is mapped to that room detects there was any activity (people count, non-call presentation, audio call, video call, or any combination of these room activities) between 9:00 AM - 10:00 AM, then it is classified as an Attended Meeting. 8 Attended Meeting Minute The sum of any room activity minutes during scheduled meeting minutes for the reporting period. For example, if a meeting is scheduled in a room (Exchange Resource ID) from 9:00 AM - 10:00 AM and the Endpoint Name (Room) that is mapped to that room detects there was any activity (people count, non-call presentation, audio call, video call, or any combination of these room activities) between 9:00 AM - 9:25 AM, then it is classified as an Attended Meeting and the Attended Meeting Minutes would be 25. 9 Scheduled Minute % (Scheduled Meeting Minute) / (Total Minutes in Reporting Period) 10 Attended Meeting Minute % (Attended Meeting Minute) / (Scheduled Meeting Minute) 11 Missed Meeting Minute The sum of scheduled minutes for Missed Meetings during the reporting period. For example, if a meeting is scheduled in a room (Exchange Resource ID) from 9:00 AM - 10:00 AM and the Endpoint Name (Room) that is mapped to that room detects there was no activity (people count, non-call presentation, audio call, video call, or any combination of these room activities) between 9:00 AM - 10:00 AM, then it is classified as a Missed Meeting and the Missed Meeting Minute value would be 60. 12 Missed Meeting Minute % (Missed Meeting Minute) / (Scheduled Meeting Minute) 13 Unused Meeting Minute % [(Scheduled Meeting Minute) - (Attended Meeting Minute)]/(Scheduled Meeting Minute) 14 Unused Meeting Minute (Scheduled Meeting Minute) - (Attended Meeting Minute) 15 Attended Meeting % (Attended Meeting Count) / (Scheduled Meeting Count) 16 Missed % (Missed Meeting Count) / (Scheduled Meeting Count) 17 Missed Meeting Count The count of scheduled meetings during the reporting period in which no WXP Collaboration monitored activity was detected between the start and end of the scheduled meeting. For example, if a meeting is scheduled in a room (Exchange Resource ID) from 9:00 AM - 10:00 AM and the Endpoint Name (Room) that is mapped to that room detects no activity (people count, non-call presentation, audio call, video call, or any combination of these room activities) between 9:00 AM - 10:00 AM, then it is classified as a Missed Meeting . 18 Max. Room Capacity The maximum number of people the room is designed for. This value is uploaded to WXP Collaboration via CSV upload. Please contact your WXP Collaboration team for instructions. 19 No Activity Minute The number of minutes during the reporting period in which there is zero activity based on a 24-hour day (1440), not the Utilization Period. 20 Occupied Room Minute The number of minutes during the reporting period in which any room activity is collected (people count, local presentation, audio call, video call) based on a 24-hour day (1440), not the Utilization Period 21 No Activity % (No Activity Minutes) / (Total Minutes in reporting period) 22 Occupied Room % (Occupied Room Minutes) / (Total Minutes in reporting period) 23 Average Total People Count The average of all people count readings for the reporting period (include zeros, exclude “-1” where People Count is temporarily unavailable). 24 Average Occupied People Count This is the average of all people count readings during the reporting period when there is at least one person in the room. 25 Max. People Count The highest single-person count reading during the reporting period. 26 Occupancy Utilization % (Occupied Room Minutes) / (Utilization Period Minutes) 27 Average Total Capacity % Average of all capacity rate readings where rate = [(People count) / (Max Room Capacity)] * 100. 28 Average Occupied Capacity % Average of "capacity rates" during the reporting period when people count >= 1, where "Capacity rate" equals [(People count) / (Max Room Capacity) ] * 100. 29 In-Call Presentation Minute The number of minutes during the reporting period in which the endpoint mapped to the room is presenting during a call. This includes both audio and video calls. 30 Non-Call Presentation Minute The number of minutes during the reporting period in which the endpoint mapped to the room is presenting while no call is taking place on that endpoint. 31 Presentation Utilization % (Presentation Minutes) / (Utilization Period Minutes) 32 Call Presentation Utilization % (In-Call Presentation Minutes) / (Utilization Period Minutes) 33 Non-Call Presentation Utilization % (Non-Call Presentation Minutes) / (Utilization Period Minutes) 34 Video Call Count The count of video calls made during the reporting period. 35 Video Call Minute The total minutes of video calls during the reporting period. 36 Audio Call Count The count of audio calls made during the reporting period. 37 Audio Call Minute The total minutes of audio calls during the reporting period. 38 Presentation Count The count of presentations made during the reporting period (includes in-call and non-call). 39 Presentation Minute The number of minutes during the reporting period during which the endpoint in the room is presenting. This includes both in-call and non-call presentation minutes. 40 People Only Count The count of occurrences during the reporting period when a person or multiple people were in a room without using any room technology monitored by WXP Collaboration. For example, if two people walk into a room and have a no-tech one-on-one meeting for 30 minutes, that would be a 1-People-Only Count room activity. 41 People Only Minute The number of minutes during the reporting period when a person or multiple people were in a room without using any room technology monitored by WXP Collaboration. For example, if two people walk into a room and have a no-tech one-on-one meeting for 30 minutes, that would be 30 People Only Minutes of room activity. 42 Presentation Only Count The count of occurrences during the reporting period when a presentation is delivered on the endpoint, when there is no other room activity taking place across what is monitored by WXP Collaboration in that room. For example, when two people walk into a room and collaborate for 30 minutes on a presentation that's shared locally but do not make a call, that would be 1 Presentation Only Count room activity. 43 Presentation Only Minute The number of minutes during the reporting period when a presentation is delivered on the endpoint, when there is no other room activity taking place across what is monitored by WXP Collaboration in that room. For example, when two people walk into a room and collaborate for 30 minutes on a presentation that's shared locally but do not make a call, that would be 30 Presentation Only Minutes of room activity. 44 Video Only Count The count of occurrences during the reporting period when a video call is made in a room. This room activity count does not include occurrences when there is both a video and a presentation. 45 Video Only Minute The number of minutes during the reporting period when a video call is made in a room. This room activity count does not include minutes when there is both a video and a presentation. 46 Audio Only Count The count of occurrences during the reporting period when an audio call is made in a room. This room activity count does not include occurrences when there is both audio and presentation. 47 Audio Only Minute The number of minutes during the reporting period when an audio call is made in a room. This room activity count does not include minutes when there is both audio and presentation. 48 Presentation & Video Count The count of occurrences during the reporting period when a video call is made in a room that also includes presentation. 49 Presentation & Video Minute The number of minutes during the reporting period when a video call is made in a room that is also sharing a presentation on that call. 50 Presentation & Audio Count The count of occurrences during the reporting period when an audio call is made in a room that also includes a presentation. 51 Presentation & Audio Minute The number of minutes during the reporting period when an audio call is made in a room that is also sharing a presentation on that call. 52 No Activity Count The number of occurrences during the reporting period in which there was no activity measured in the room across all aspects WXP Collaboration was monitoring. For example, if the reporting period was one day and there was nobody in the room from 12:00 AM to 9:00 AM, then an audio call was made from 9:00 AM - 10:00 AM, then nobody was in the room the rest of the day, the "No Activity Count" for that day would be 2 from the 12:00 AM - 9:00 AM occurrence plus the 10:00 AM - 11:59 PM occurrence. 53 The number of minutes during the reporting period in which there was no activity measured in the room across all aspects WXP Collaboration was monitoring. For example, if the reporting period was one day and there was nobody in the room from 12:00 AM to 9:00 AM, then an audio call was made from 9:00 AM - 10:00 AM, then nobody was in the room the rest of the day, the "No Activity Minute" for that day would be 1,380 from the 540 minutes from 12:00 AM - 9:00 AM plus the 840 minutes 10:00 AM - 11:59 PM occurrence. 54 Scheduled Room Activity Minute Room activity that took place during a scheduled meeting (Room Activity Does not include "No Activity") 55 Non-Scheduled Room Activity Minute Room activity that took place when no meeting was scheduled (ad-hoc) (Room Activity Does not include "No Activity").

items.heading: Enhancing WXP Collaboration’s “Quality” Metrics
items.text: We are excited to announce that we have made significant improvements to our “Quality” Metrics. These changes are designed to more accurately reflect true user experiences and provide you with more confidence in taking action on your data. The changes include individualized quality thresholds for Audio, Video, and Presentation channels. This eliminates some “false positive” quality reporting that was previously caused by issues on the Presentation channel. Additionally, latency is now factored into the quality data when provided by the source. All quality thresholds (Good / Fair / Bad) have also been updated to more accurately reflect true user experiences. We have also improved the reporting of Zoom quality metrics. Previously, Zoom quality issues were being under-reported in Tech Insights Analytics, but over-reported in Tech Insights Monitoring. The new metrics for Zoom are a more accurate reflection of the user quality experience.

Tech Insights Analytics and Monitoring are reporting results using the new metrics effective Jan 22, 2020 What you will see at the transition is that all customers will experience differing levels of quality reporting changes, given the new definitions. The number and percentages of calls, meetings, and participant experience reported at each threshold are likely to change. If you have alerts based on quality, the frequency of those alerts will also change. The magnitude of the change is very specific to your configuration and the levels of quality that currently exist across your infrastructure and endpoints. Generally, you will see fewer Bad calls, meetings, and participants in your reporting and alerting. Bad calls may be lower by 25%-40% (Median 33%); Bad Meetings may be lower by 1%-15% (Median 7%); and Bad Participants may be lower by 1%-20% (Median 7%). To ensure that you are taking advantage of these improvements, we recommend that you run any regular quality reports and compare them to prior reports to understand the changes for your unique configuration. Additionally, review and adjust Quality alerting levels as needed. We understand that changes in reporting of core metrics, such as quality, are challenging, especially when that reporting has been shared and made actionable across our customers’ organizations. At the same time, we are committed to evolving the entire science of Unified Communication quality reporting across the industry.

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: The Bookings dataset in Workspace Insights combines calendar reservations with meeting room and device usage data. It shows what was scheduled and what actually happened in each space. You can see who books meeting rooms most or least, how fully spaces are used during the scheduled time, which recurring meetings are not attended, and where occupancy is often near capacity. These insights support space planning, safety and compliance, cleaning priorities, and overall workplace efficiency.
This dataset helps you analyze: This dataset is available to Workspace Insights customers. If you do not have access and would like to learn more, contact your Account Manager.

items.heading: Column reference
items.text: Data points Description Applicable systems Comments Scheduled Meeting ID The unique identifier of the scheduled meeting as designated by the collection source. Scheduled Meeting Name The name of the scheduled meeting that is set by the organizer and seen by all invitees. Organizer ID The ID (usually an email address) of the organizer of the scheduled meeting. Recurring Indicates whether the scheduled meeting is part of a recurring series (Yes) or a single instance (No). Start Time The start time of the scheduled meeting (date-time). End Time The end time of the scheduled meeting (date-time). Start Hour The hour the scheduled meeting started, as a single number (0–23) based on the organization’s time zone. Exchange Resource ID (Meeting) The unique identifier of the space that the scheduled meeting reserved. Room Name The name of the room associated with the unique room key (usually the Exchange Resource ID). The source of the room name is either the WXP Collaboration Exchange integration or a client CSV upload. Attended Indicates whether the scheduled meeting was attended (Yes) or missed (No). Determined by matching room activity collected from the endpoint mapped to the room with the calendar data. Booking Minutes The allotted time for the scheduled meeting (minutes). Attended Booking Minutes Minutes between Start Time and End Time during which any activity was detected by the endpoint associated with the space. Example: For a 60-minute meeting from 1:00–2:00 p.m. where the room was used from 1:00–1:25 p.m., Attended Booking Minutes = 25. Missed Booking Minutes If a booking is missed, this equals the Booking Minutes (the duration of missed bookings). Unused Booking Minutes Minutes between Start Time and End Time during which no activity was detected by the endpoint associated with the room. Example: For a 60-minute meeting from 1:00–2:00 p.m. where the room was used from 1:00–1:25 p.m., Unused Booking Minutes = 35. Fully missed meetings are included (for a 60-minute missed meeting, Unused Booking Minutes = 60). Occupied Room Capacity Between the Start Time and End Time of the booking, the average value of People Count divided by Maximum Room Capacity when People Count > 0. Endpoints that support people counting Requires non‑zero Maximum Capacity on the room. Avg People Count Per Meeting The average number of people in the room during the scheduled meeting, as determined by the endpoint’s people-counting feature. Applicable when people counting is supported. Max People Count Per Meeting The maximum number of people in the room during the scheduled meeting, as determined by the endpoint’s people-counting feature.

items.heading: Bookings dataset KPIs
items.text: KPI selection Default Tooltip results Unique Scheduled Meeting Count Yes The unique count of scheduled meetings during the reporting period. A scheduled meeting is a calendar event with at least one space resource reserved. A scheduled meeting can have one or many bookings. Total Bookings The count of bookings during the period. Unique Organizer Count The unique count of organizers in the bookings dataset for the reporting period. Unique Room Count The unique count of rooms in the bookings dataset for the reporting period. Recurring Booking Count No The sum of bookings during the reporting period where Recurring = “Yes.” This does not de-duplicate a series. Average Start Hour The average of start hours (0–23) for all bookings during the period, based on the organization’s time zone. Attended Count The count of bookings where Attended = “Yes.” Missed Count The count of bookings where Attended = “No.” Total Booking Minutes The sum of Booking Minutes for all bookings during the reporting period. Average Booking Minutes The average of Booking Minutes for all bookings during the reporting period. Total Attended Booking Minutes The sum of Attended Booking Minutes during the reporting period. Average Attended Booking Minutes The average of Attended Booking Minutes during the reporting period. Total Missed Booking Minutes The sum of Missed Booking Minutes during the reporting period. Average Missed Booking Minutes The average of Missed Booking Minutes during the reporting period. Total Unused Booking Minutes The sum of Unused Booking Minutes during the reporting period. Average Unused Booking Minutes The average of Unused Booking Minutes during the reporting period. Average Occupied Booking Capacity The average of Occupied Booking Capacity readings for all bookings during the reporting period.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Difference Between a Meeting and a Call in WXP Collaboration

items.heading: Difference Between a Meeting and a Call in WXP Collaboration
Introduction
items.text: HP WXP Collaboration defines a “meeting” as a real or virtual place where two or more people join in the same space. Tech Insights Analytics in the WXP Collaboration application shows meeting details by assembling and matching raw call detail records for calls with multiple participants that share a common destination address (such as a VMR) and occur during the same timeframe. In simple terms, a meeting is usually made up of multiple calls and call legs. The Start Time is the earliest time a participant joined the meeting, and the End Time is the latest time a participant left. Meeting quality is shown based on the “worst-case” quality for any participant. This means the participant with the highest packet loss and/or jitter determines the overall meeting quality. If even one participant experienced poor quality, the meeting quality is displayed as Bad. In the example below, the meeting Start Time is 10/31/2016 09:39:55, which is when the first participant joined. The End Time is 10/31/2016 10:54:14, which is when the last participant (the second participant) disconnected. The overall quality is shown as “Fair.” Although the first participant had a good experience, the second participant experienced fair quality, which determines the quality displayed for the meeting.

To help users of WXP Collaboration application identify call quality issues quickly and speed up troubleshooting, all individual call legs that are currently experiencing bad quality can be viewed in the Calls tab in Tech Insights Monitoring. If a call is using VMR or bridge technology, the entire meeting is visible in the Meetings tab. By clicking the meeting nam, you can view additional details including the participants and the quality they are currently experiencing. For more information on Tech Insights Monitoring, please refer to the article - Tech Insights Monitoring Overview.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Comparing Tech Insights Monitoring and Tech Insights Analytics Data

items.heading: Comparing Tech Insights Monitoring and Tech Insights Analytics Data
Introduction
items.text: The reports and displays from Tech Insights Monitoring and Tech Insights Analytics are not expected to be identical. This is because each module captures different types of data based on the reporting and insights they are designed to provide.

items.heading: Common Scenarios Where the Data May Not Match
items.text: Tech Insights Monitoring and Tech Insights Analytics obtain data from different sources. Tech Insights Monitoring uses real-time data feeds from endpoints and infrastructure, while Tech Insights Analytics uses historical data such as call records and system configuration. Because of these differences, mismatches may occur, particularly when either module has delays or gaps in updating.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Monitoring Live Call Quality in WXP Collaboration

items.heading: Monitoring Live Call Quality in WXP Collaboration
Introduction
items.text: HP WXP Collaboration provides real-time visibility into active calls and meetings. This feature enables IT administrators to detect quality issues such as packet loss and jitter as they occur. This article explains how to monitor the quality of a call that is currently in progress. You can monitor active calls from the Calls, Meetings, and Endpoints tabs.

items.heading: Calls Tab
items.text: In the Calls tab under Datasets, you can view all active call legs monitored through your call controls, bridges, and management systems. You can view detailed quality information by clicking the Quality link in the Quality (Last known) column.
Clickin Quality opens a window that shows details such as channel rate, packet loss, and jitter for audio, video, and presentation channels.

items.heading: Meetings Tab
items.text: In the Meetings tab, you can view all active multi‑participant meetings monitored through your call controls, bridges, and management systems. Meeting quality is displayed based on the “worst-case” quality experienced by any participant. That is, the participant with the highest packet loss or jitter determines the overall meeting quality shown. Clicking the meeting name displays all participants and their corresponding quality information.

items.heading: Endpoints Tab
items.text: In the Endpoints tab, you can view all endpoints currently in use. If WXP Collaboration Endpoint Monitoring feature is enabled, any endpoint that is on a call appears as a hyperlink.
Clicking the endpoint name opens the endpoint details view. Selecting the Calls sub‑tab directs you to Datasets > Calls, where you can view the call’s quality information.
Clicking on the Quality link will give you additional information regarding the call such as channel rate, packet loss, and jitter for your audio, video, and presentation channels.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Understanding the Difference Between Participants and Unique Participants

items.heading: Understanding the Difference Between Participants and Unique Participants
Question
items.text: What is the difference between the value in participants and unique participants in Tech Insights Analytics?

items.heading: Answer
items.text: The meaning of these two terms depends on the data being examined:

items.heading: Per Meeting
items.text: In a per-meeting context, the Unique Participant is the entity that participated in the meeting, regardless of how many times they joined and left. In the same context, any call into the meeting will count as a Participant - for example: Example: A meeting takes place with 8 people; there are therefore 8 Unique Participants. During the course of the call, two people dropped their connections and rejoined. There were therefore 10 Participants, but still only 8 Unique Participants.

items.heading: Data Covering a Time Interval
items.text: When dealing with data specified by a time interval, the term Unique Participant refers to each party seen once or more times during the interval. Participant, however, refers to the total count of parties (whether repeated or not) over the time interval.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Why do I See Multiple Calls in Tech Insights Monitoring When Only One is Expected?

items.heading: Why do I See Multiple Calls in Tech Insights Monitoring When Only One is Expected?
items.text: Question: I have made a single call, but I see multiple calls displayed in Tech Insights Monitoring. Why? Answer: HP WXP Collaboration displays detailed call information in Tech Insights Monitoring to help administrators diagnose issues at the call-leg level. Tech Insights Monitoring does not consolidate calls; instead, it shows each call leg separately. This allows issues to be narrowed down with greater precision. As a result, multiple parts of the same call may appear as separate entries. Tech Insights Analytics works differently. Its focus is on metrics rather than diagnostics, so multiple call legs are consolidated wherever possible. In this module, the same call usually appears as a single entry.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Why am I missing meetings from Webex in Tech Insights Monitoring?

items.heading: Why am I missing meetings from Webex in Tech Insights Monitoring?
items.text: Why am I not able to see some of the meetings from Webex in HP WXP collaboration > Tech Insights Monitoring? Answer: The current Cisco Webex Collector has a few known issues when collecting meeting data from Control Hub. Meeting data may take up to 10 minutes to become available through the Cisco APIs, and participant quality data may take up to 5 additional minutes to appear. Because of this, it can take up to 15 minutes for a Webex meeting to appear in Tech Insights Monitoring. Meetings that are shorter than 15 minutes may not appear in Tech Insights Monitoring because the meeting and participant data may not be available in time for retrieval through the monitoring API. Note: Due to these limitations, meeting data from Webex is not guaranteed unless the meeting lasts at least 15 minutes.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Understanding the “For (Minutes)” Setting in WXP Collaboration Tech Insights Alerts

items.heading: Understanding the “For (Minutes)” Setting in WXP Collaboration Tech Insights Alerts
Introduction
items.text: This article explains how the For (Minutes) setting controls the continuous fault duration required before an alert is triggered in WXP Collaboration’s Tech Insights Monitoring, helping reduce false or transient alerts.

items.heading: The For (Minutes) Field Setting
items.text: The For (Minutes) field is an important part of setting up an alert in WXP Collaboration's Tech Insights Monitoring. This field sets the continuous fault duration required to trigger an alert. Tech Insights Monitoring is constantly retrieving status information and activity data from your monitored UC environment, and typically receives updates every 15 seconds for every dimension that can be set for Alerts. The minimum setting for the For (Minutes) field is 1 minute, meaning 4 consecutive measurements of the monitored value would have to be at or beyond the alert threshold before the alert will be sent. (2 minutes = 8 consecutive alert level values, 5 minutes = 20 consecutive alert level values, etc.) If the value returns to a normal range before the alert is triggered, then the duration “counter” is reset, and any subsequent alert level reading would again be subject to the full continuous fault duration once again. For more information on setting up alerts, please refer to the following articles:

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Registering for the Daily Data Collection Status Report

items.heading: Registering for the Daily Data Collection Status Report
Introduction
items.text: This article explains how to register for the Daily Data Collection Status Report to receive automated updates on WXP Collaboration data collection health. The report helps you identify potential issues early and take action before they impact your environment.

items.heading: Q. WXP Collaboration Produces A Daily Collection Health Report - How do I Receive This?
items.text: A. In order to receive the daily report, just register as a Technical Contact for your organisation. This is done using the WXP Collaboration Administration Portal. Once logged in, go to Account Settings:
In Account Settings, select ADD CONTACT to open a form to complete.
From the Type dropdown, select ‘Technical’ and complete the other details accordingly.
Any email address associated with a Technical Contact will receive the status email.

items.heading: Q. What is the Purpose of the Report?
items.text: A. The report attempts to provide an early indication of potential problems with WXP Collaboration data collection in your environment so that action can be taken to correct issues.

items.heading: Q. I have a system showing an error, but there is no issue. Why is this?
items.text: A. When devices have not supplied data for some time, it is flagged first as a warning and then as an error, along with the date of the last seen data. If the device, for instance, is not carrying traffic, then it may be flagged as having collection issues. Use your knowledge of your environment, along with the report, to determine if action needs to be taken

items.heading: Q. Can I Have the Report Delivered to Microsoft Teams/Slack, etc.?
items.text: A. The report can be delivered to any system that will accept an email - the content is standard HTML. For Microsoft Teams, take the following steps Select the channel you wish to use to see the notifications, click on the ... at the right-hand side, and then click on Get mail address

For Slack, proceed as follows: Select the Slack channel you wish to use. Click on the dropdown indicator (∨) (alongside the channel name, select Integrations, and then select 'Send emails to this channel'.
Follow the prompts, and you will be given an email address to use as the Technical Contact.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Leveraging Intelligent Monitoring and Alerts to Create Custom Collector Down Notifications

items.heading: Leveraging Intelligent Monitoring and Alerts to Create Custom Collector Down Notifications
Introduction
items.text: Intelligent Monitoring enables you to proactively track the health of your WXP Collaboration Data Collector by creating alerts that notify you via email whenever a collector goes down, helping you respond quickly and minimize monitoring gaps. If you have an on-premises data collector to monitor your on-premises infrastructure (Cisco, Polycom, Pexip, etc.), you will need to monitor it to ensure it stays online and captures all your valuable meeting and call data. One of the most common problems customers run into is when the data collector is taken down, network connectivity causes connectivity issues to our cloud, customer-deployed environment change, which all prevent us from collecting your systems’ data. With our new release of Intelligent Monitoring and Alerts, we have added the ability to monitor the state of your on-premises collector. We can track the number of real-time calls (streams) currently being collected from your on-premises collector, and if we see a drop in your active calls, we can send you an alert. Not only will we send an alert, but we can also show you the history of the streams to show when potential collector problems started. Finally, you can direct the alert to your team (or its members) and include instructions on how to take action (which you can change anytime). Gone are the days when an alert comes in about a collector issue, and nothing happens since the recipient is on vacation.

items.heading: Set up a Critical Alert for the Environment
items.text: Here is how you can set up your Critical Alert for your environment today. NoteIf you are using WXP Collaboration to monitor only UCaaS systems like Zoom, Webex, and Microsoft Teams, you won't need to set up a collector alert, since all UCaaS systems are collected from the WXP Collaboration Cloud. Note If you are using WXP Collaboration to monitor only UCaaS systems like Zoom, Webex, and Microsoft Teams, you won't need to set up a collector alert, since all UCaaS systems are collected from the WXP Collaboration Cloud. The Intelligent monitoring rules can be found by navigating to Technology Insights Monitoring -> Monitors -> Rules Tab to see all the Rules created for Intelligent monitoring. You can see the list of all monitors in the organization, including each monitor's current state.

By clicking on the ‘Name’, you can edit the parameters of an existing Intelligent monitoring. Creating a monitoring alert consists of the following steps: In order to create an Intelligent monitoring alert, click ‘Add monitor’ and provide a name for the alert that best describes your Alert. In this example, we will create an alert to monitor the status of the WXP Collaboration Data Collector by checking whether WXP Collaboration is receiving any call data. The absence of all call data would indicate a possible problem with your collector if this occurs during expected working hours. Name: “Critical - Collector Down Alert”
The first Section: Define the Metric

Once the Category and metric have been selected, you can add any additional filters as needed. To create a Collector down alert, we will be adding the following filter: “Data Source does not contain WEBEX, and Data Source does not contain GMEET, and Data Source does not contain TEAMS, and Data Source does not contain BJN, and Data Source does not contain ZOOM." The above UCaaS data sources are excluded because this data is not gathered by your on-premises data collector; instead, it is collected on your behalf by collectors in the WXP Collaboration Cloud.

Once the metrics have been defined, Set the alert conditions for which you want the alert to be triggered. To create a Collector down alert, select the following values. This alert condition means – When the Call streams are equal to 0 for consecutive 30 minutes, the WXP Collaboration Data collector is down.

Operator: The metric is compared against the thresholds using the user-input operator. There are 6 options - “Greater than or Equals”, “Greater than”, “Less than”, “Less than or Equals”, “Equals”, “Not Equals”. This is a mandatory field. Thresholds: User inputted field(s) the metric is compared to with the operator. Critical is meant to signify the most severe, then warn, then info as the least severe. All thresholds are optional, but each monitor must have at least one threshold filled in. The Notify section is enabled for each threshold level only after a value is entered. Duration: The user inputted value (in minutes) that the metric must satisfy the comparison of the operator vs threshold in order to count trigger a change of monitor state. This is a mandatory field. Once the Alert conditions are set, select the details to include in the notification sent to your team. You can create different notifications to be sent for ‘Critical, Warn, and Info’ status.

Enable Email Per Threshold: Checking this box means an email will be sent when that threshold’s state is reached. Unchecking the box will not send an email. Note, unchecking does not invalidate that state from the “current state” live monitoring - you’ll still see it, just no email. Subject: This is the subject of the email being sent. Note that it can be the same or different from the monitor name. We will automatically append the threshold state before the subject (i.e., “[Critical] This is the email subject” in the above example). To (Recipient): This contains the emails to which the alert will be sent. When selecting the recipient, you will want someone on the list who will be able to take action on the alert once they receive it Send As: Signifies the type of email being delivered. The three options are “HTML”, “HTML - Custom”, and “Plain Text”. “HTML - Custom” allows an additional input to customize the text in the email body. An example of the email that gets sent out is shown below: We haven't received any active calls with the WXP Collaboration Data Collector located on the server "ABC" over the last 30 minutes. If you have received this alert, please check that the VyoptaCPMDataCollector service is up and running. NoteThis knowledge base article will guide you through the steps that is needed to restart the collector. This knowledge base article will guide you through the steps that is needed to restart the collector. Frequency: Currently under evaluation for inclusion, removal, or update, given that it is illogical under the “email send on change of state” guidance we are operating under. Days & Active Schedule: These are the user-inputted values for when emails should fire. In this example, the alerts would only send an email Monday through Friday, 12 am to 12 pm PST. All state changes outside of that schedule would still be tracked in the UI, but no email would be sent. You should be aware, however, that when an alert becomes 'active' by a schedule, the criteria are applied immediately:- therefore, should an alert be scheduled to become active at 08:00, and examines a 30-minute window (duration) data will be used for the preceding 30 minutes - thus if the alert would have been triggered with the data from 07:30 - 08:00 then an alert will be sent. To avoid this, set the scheduled activation time to be one duration interval after the time that measurement should start - in this case, this would be 08:00 plus 30 minutes, or 08:30 NoteIn this example we have used a calls metric to alert us when the WXP Collaboration Data Collector may be down. Depending upon your environment and expected working hours it may be appropriate to use a different metric such as monitoring Call controls or other infrastructure status. In this example we have used a calls metric to alert us when the WXP Collaboration Data Collector may be down. Depending upon your environment and expected working hours it may be appropriate to use a different metric such as monitoring Call controls or other infrastructure status.

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: This article explains how to interpret critical Actionable Alerts from WXP Collaboration and what steps UC engineers should take when an alert is triggered.

items.heading: Critical Alerts
items.text: When users receive alerts from WXP Collaboration, they must understand what they mean and what actions are required to resolve them. As a recipient of the critical alert, I’m unclear about what it indicates, whether it truly requires action, and how to resolve the issue to stay ahead of user complaints and minimize endpoint downtime.

items.heading: Solution
items.text: If you are a UC engineer and received this critical alert from the WXP Collaboration monitoring engine, you need to take some action. When you get one of these alerts, you should be able to answer the following questions at first:

items.heading: Group By - Nothing
items.text: When we do not supply a Group By value, a single notification will be sent when the alert conditions are met. This is the default behavior of WXP Collaboration alerts. Once Actionable Alerts are enabled for your organization, you will see that your existing alerts will show ‘Nothing’ in the Group By dropdown. The Group by Nothing page is the single notification when the alert conditions are met; in this example, we are using all call streams and sending an alert if we stop seeing any call streams for more than 5 minutes.1)Set filter conditions for these calls, Filter and Search. 2) Define Status Trigger(s) Set and add multiple triggers for Critical, Warn, and Info.
Here’s an example of how the alert email appears:

items.heading: Group By Simple
items.text: Separate notification for each Call/Endpoint when the alert trigger conditions are met. This Group By condition will send a separate notification for each call or endpoint when the alert conditions are met. So if you have three endpoints in the alert, and the alert condition is met for two of them, you will receive two emails (one for each endpoint). When we select Endpoints, we can either set a dynamic filter as shown or select individual endpoints.1)Trend Chart updates to show the multiple Calls/Endpoints. 2)Group by “Simple” also simplifies the status trigger by locking down the Metric Value to 0.

items.heading: Group By Advanced
items.text: Separate notifications for each group when the alert trigger conditions are met. If you selected Model type, had two DX80’s in the same group, and both met the alert condition, you would receive a single email for both DX80’s. You can choose from the multiple available options for each metric. In the example below, we have selected Model and tags. Similar to the Single group by, we will now start grouping models and tags. If multiple endpoints start raising alerts, we will only send an alert for all endpoints that raise it for each model and tag.
To know more about Tech Insights Monitoring rules, you can refer to the article: New in Tech Insights Monitoring - Intelligent Monitoring Engine

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: What Does the For (Minutes) Field on Alerts Mean?

items.heading: What Does the For (Minutes) Field on Alerts Mean?
items.text: The For (Minutes) field is an important part of setting up an alert in Vyopta's Tech Insights Monitoring. This field sets the continuous fault duration required to trigger an alert. Tech Insights Monitoring is constantly retrieving status information and activity data from your monitored UC environment, and typically receives updates every 15 seconds for every dimension that can be set for Alerts. The minimum setting for the For (Minutes) field is 1 minute, meaning 4 consecutive measurements of the monitored value would have to be at or beyond the alert threshold before the alert will be sent. (2 minutes = 8 consecutive alert level values, 5 minutes = 20 consecutive alert level values, etc.) If the value returns to a normal range before the alert is triggered, then the duration “counter” is reset, and any subsequent alert level reading would again be subject to the full continuous fault duration once again. For more information on setting up alerts, please refer to the following articles:

items.heading: Stopping Alerts After Removing a Touch panel from an Endpoint
Question
items.text: I have removed a peripheral from my endpoint. How do I stop alerts from being triggered?

items.heading: Answer
items.text: When an endpoint detects that a peripheral has been connected, the status is held in the WXP Collaboration configuration. If that peripheral is removed, WXP Collaboration triggers any configured alerts to notify you that it is missing. There are times, however, when a peripheral is intentionally removed, and alerts should not be generated. In these cases, once the peripheral has been removed, it should be deleted from the endpoint's configuration. In either case, a display similar to the one below will be shown
Click on the dustbin symbol to delete the peripheral. A warning will be shown as follows:
To display the updated endpoint information, you must re-display the endpoint; the configuration information will not be updated dynamically.

items.heading: Setting Up Webhooks for Alert Notifications
Introduction
items.text: Webhooks let Tech Insights Monitoring send alert notifications directly to external systems in real time. By posting a small JSON payload to a publicly accessible URL, you can create tickets in your ITSM tool, post messages to chat channels (for example, Microsoft Teams, Slack, or Webex), or trigger automated remediation steps. This helps to reduce manual checking, decreases incident response time, and centralizes communication. This article provides steps-by-step guidance for setting up webhooks for notifications.

items.heading: Using the Correct URL
items.text: If you've selected one of the preset templates (for example, MS Teams Notification or Slack), a webhook URL needs to be generated on the Microsoft Teams or Slack. If you're configuring a webhook for a third-party system, please see how that system accepts webhooks.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Classification of Bridge Call Quality as Good, Fair, and Bad

items.heading: Classification of Bridge Call Quality as Good, Fair, and Bad
Introduction
items.text: Bridge call quality helps administrators understand how packet loss and jitter affect user experience during multiparty calls. HP WXP Collaboration classifies bridge calls as Good, Fair, or Bad. This feature allows you to evaluate service performance, identify trends, and troubleshoot issues that may affect call reliability.

items.heading: Bridge Call Quality Thresholds
items.text: WXP Collaboration defines Bad quality as a level of interference from packet loss or jitter that is likely to be noticeable to users on an average hardware endpoint. Thresholds are based on input from manufacturers, user feedback, and analysis of large dataset available to WXP Collaboration.

items.heading: Analyzing Bridge Call Quality
items.text: Analyze bridge call quality in the Issues tab of Tech Insights Analytics using the following two panels:
Note: On these panels, you have several options for reviewing the data. For both quality charts, each of the quality values can be excluded or re-included by clicking on the respective legend entry.

items.heading: Reviewing Trends
items.text: On these panels, you have several options for reviewing the data. For both quality charts mentioned above, each of the quality values can be excluded or re-included by clicking on the respective legend. The Bridge Participant Quality Trend panel groups data based on the selected date range. For example, when viewing three months of data, you can group results by month, week, or day. Additional options, such as Quarter, appear when the date range is expanded. You may include or exclude specific quality values by selecting the corresponding legend entry. When a category such as “Unknown” is removed, the panels update automatically.

items.heading: Interactive Quality Pie Chart
items.text: The Bridge Participant Quality pie chart is interactive. To drill into any calls that for a particular bridge quality, you can click on that reason portion in the pie chart.
Selecting a quality segment, such as Bad section of the pie (shown in red), opens the Participants view within the Meetings tab, which shows all participants who experienced that quality level.

items.heading: Viewing Participant Details
items.text: To view more detail for a specific participant, click the Participant Address in the Participants view.

Note: Use the date range picker in the upper-right corner of any tab in Tech Insights Analytics to customize the analysis period. You can also use the share or forward icon to create a one-time or recurring report.

items.heading: Overview of Poly Lens Add-on in WXP
Introduction
items.text: Poly Lens is an Add-on feature in HP Workforce Experience (WXP) to enable organizations to connect their WXP tenant with a Poly Lens Cloud tenant. This integration allows IT administrators to streamlined, click-through workflow that opens Poly Lens Cloud in a new browser where you can deploy, remotely monitor, update, and troubleshoot your collaboration devices and rooms inventory, with deeper visibility into performance and health. This helps you stay ahead of issues, simplify day-to-day management, and deliver consistent experiences everywhere your team’s work. The Poly Lens Add-on in WXP focuses on authorization and seamless navigation, while management of hybrid collaboration spaces, rooms, and devices continues to be performed directly in Poly Lens Cloud. For detailed instructions on onboarding and managing Poly devices, organizing rooms, monitoring device health, and reviewing insights, refer to the Poly Lens Cloud Help.

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: The Home page of the Workforce Experience Platform (WXP) provides a centralized dashboard for you to gain insights into the digital experience of your employees. Regularly monitoring the dashboard helps you to proactively address potential issues before they impact employee productivity. The Home page provides a comprehensive view of your organization’s digital experience. The data is presented across the following sections or widgets:

items.heading: Understanding the Dashboard
items.text: The data is presented across multiple widgets or sections as follows:

items.heading: Experience Score
items.text: The Experience Score section displays a numeric score representing the digital experience, ranging from 0 to 100, of employees. This score is determined by combining Sentiment data (employee feedback from surveys) and Telemetry data (system performance metrics). For additional information, refer to the detailed article - Understanding Digital Experience Score.

items.heading: Experience Over Time
items.text: This section provides a line graph of experience trends across a selected timeframe, such as the last 30, 60, or 90 days. It helps IT administrators assess whether workforce experience is improving or declining. The X-axis shows the date, and the Y-axis shows the number of devices. Experience scores are categorized into categories, such asGreat (green line), Fair (orange line), and Poor (red line). By monitoring Experience Over Time, IT teams can identify recurring issues and take proactive measures to improve digital experiences.

items.heading: Alerts
items.text: The Alerts section highlights critical security and system health issues detected across your device fleet. Alerts are categorized into severity levels, such as Critical, High, Medium, and Low.
For additional information, refer to Overview of Alerts and Configuring Alerts.

items.heading: Fleet Inventory
items.text: The Fleet Inventory section provides an overview of devices across the organization, categorized by type: The donut chart for PCs visually represents the device distribution across different manufacturers. IT administrators can use the Fleet Inventory to monitor device assets, identify manufacturer-specific trends, and ensure hardware remains compliant and up to date.

items.heading: Apps with Most Crashes
items.text: This section displays applications that frequently crash. Each row shows: Clicking on any app crash row navigates to the Analytics page, where IT teams can view additional details, such as: By monitoring application crashes, IT teams can prevent system disruptions and enhance employee productivity.

items.heading: Meeting Quality
items.text: This section provides insights into virtual meeting performance by tracking the number of participants across meeting apps such as: A bar chart visually represents the performance of meeting applications. This enables IT teams to identify performance issues affecting virtual meetings.

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: The Analytics feature in the HP Workforce Experience Platform (WXP) offers actionable insights into device performance, security, sustainability, inventory, and more. By analyzing data collected from enrolled devices, the platform supports proactive IT management, to improve the employee experience with digital tools at the workplace. These reports are automatically refreshed daily (with some updating more frequently) and are powered by machine learning, preconfigured logic, and contextual data. Currently, the Analytics reports are categorized as Modern Reports and Classic Reports. In upcoming releases, all Classic Reports will be modernized to align with WXP standards.

items.text: The dashboard offers a curated set of reports for IT visibility. System Health Analyzes battery, disk, and GPU health to support device stability and reliability. Network Health Monitors network connectivity, signal strength, and authentication for seamless performance. Device Utilization Reports on CPU, memory, and software usage to identify underutilized or overburdened devices. Sustainability Highlights power consumption patterns for environmental impact and energy-saving efforts. Driver Inventory Provides an overview of drivers, their versions, and current status across enrolled devices. BIOS Inventory Displays BIOS versioning and configuration for compliance and update readiness. Blue Screen Errors Delivers real-time system crash analytics for issue resolution and trend analysis. Application Inventory Shows installed applications, versions, publishers, and device counts for software tracking and compliance. Application Experience Tracks application crashes, freezes, load times, and errors to optimize device performance and user experience. OS Performance Monitors OS crashes, CPU usage, and updates to improve device stability and user experience.

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: The Devices module of the HP Workforce Experience Platform (WXP) enables you (IT administrators) to efficiently manage your fleet of devices, including PCs, printers, and virtual machines. The Devices module supports centralized monitoring, tracking, and maintenance of devices. It also provides visibility into hardware, software, warranties, and enrollment status. You can add, view, update, and delete devices individually or in bulk. Additionally, it facilitates seamless integration with Google Admin Console to enroll and manage ChromeOS devices. Managing devices without a centralized system could lead to fragmented control, manual errors, and limited visibility into device health and compliance. The Devices module addresses these challenges by streamlining operations, enhancing security, and reducing downtime.

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: The Pulses module in the HP Workforce Experience Platform (WXP) is designed to measure and enhance employee satisfaction with the technology they rely on for daily work. Through targeted surveys, you can collect valuable feedback on how well digital tools and resources meet employee needs. Additionally, the platform includes notification functionality so you can alert employees about scheduled maintenance and outages, ensuring they stay informed and connected. By leveraging these insights and updates, you can continuously improve the overall employee experience. Note: Some features mentioned here are available only with select WXP subscription plans. There are two types of Pulses: Note: Pulse popups are supported only on Windows devices.

items.heading: Key Features
items.text: All Pulses are automatically saved, allowing you to revisit them later if you're not ready to publish. Additionally, you can duplicate any existing Pulse, saving you time by avoiding the need to re-write content. Pulses appear directly on employees' desktops. This leads to higher visibility and response rates compared to surveys pointing to external links. In addition:

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: The HP Workforce Experience Platform (WXP) is a cloud-based solution designed to enhance IT operations by providing insights into device performance, user experience, and security posture. To leverage these capabilities, a successful initial setup is essential.

items.heading: Document Scope
items.text: This guide covers the self-creation of tenant and the initial deployment of WXP. As the deployment progresses, additional tasks, such as onboarding more devices or creating new administrator accounts, may be necessary. However, these tasks are beyond the scope of this document.

items.heading: Assumptions
items.text: This document assumes that the target devices are not already managed by the Workforce Experience Platform (WXP) for other HP products, such as Proactive Insights. After a device is onboarded to WXP, the same Windows Agent supports other WXP-based solutions without requiring reinstallation.

items.heading: Abbreviations
items.text: The following abbreviations are used in this guide:

items.heading: Overview of WXP Deployment Steps
items.text: Deploying WXP requires the following steps:

items.heading: Gathering Information
items.text: Gathering all the required information before starting the process is essential to ensure a successful initial deployment. The following information should be gathered: Note: In most environments, the setup works automatically. Since WXP is deployed by IT staff, they should know if any specialized proxy settings are needed. This table summarizes the information that should be gathered before proceeding with deployment: Item Selection Deployment Region (data location and support) Country: Deployment region Administrator Account Type Microsoft or HP IDs Administrator Accounts List of admin staff and IDs Agent Deployment Method Method used (scripts, SCCM, Intune etc) Proxy Configuration Proxy configuration details if not auto-discovered or not used

items.heading: Creating WXP Cloud Management Instance
items.text: After gathering the required information, create a WXP Cloud Management instance for your organization to manage the WXP solution. The setup process is designed to allow organizations to complete the process independently without requiring assistance from HP or an HP reseller. If your organization already has an instance for Premium or Premium+ Support (Formerly Active Care), HP Wolf Protect & Trace, or an existing WXP account, you can skip this step as the same instance can be used for WXP. The primary contact for the WXP deployment will receive an email titled “Your HP e-Software Delivery Confirmation,” containing the License Key and instructions to create a new instance (also referred to as tenant) in WXP. Note: Allow 24 – 72 hours for the license key to become active. You will need this active license key to proceed with the next steps.If you own Enhanced Onboarding or Premium Support, one of our Onboarding Managers will contact you within 3 business days. Note: It is necessary to either use an existing Microsoft ID or HP ID or create a new one. If creating a new HP ID, you must verify it by email. The user ID used to create the WXP instance will become the Primary Administrator account for the instance and will have full administrative rights. Follow the guided workflow to use the ID and create the instance. Important: When filling out the form in https://workforceexperience.hp.com/signup select a Country corresponding to the preferred region. Refer to table from the Gathering Information section.
Once the instance is created, you will be taken to the Home page. It will appear empty at first. Click the Guided tour and Onboarding for the next steps.

items.heading: Creating Additional Administrator Accounts
items.text: Additional administrator accounts can be added. Refer to the article - Inviting a New User. Administrators must have the “IT Role” assigned to them. Note that this role also grants the ability to Find a device.

items.heading: Deploying HP Insights Windows Application on Target Devices
items.text: After the WXP instance is created and configured, deploy the HP Insights Windows Application or Windows Agent to your devices. Follow the steps below to install the agent with the correct settings. Note: Some organizations use MSI packages for software deployment. A full discussion on using the MSI package is outside of the scope of this document.

Note: If custom proxy settings are needed, refer to this article . If a determination on proxy settings requirements is not made, test deploying on one device without any custom proxy settings to determine if the agent connects to the WXP management platform. After completing the steps above, the agent automatically connects to WXP and onboards to the WXP instance associated with the Company PIN. The device does not need to reboot, but the agent may take up to 15 minutes to fully deploy and synchronize with the WXP instance. Once the process is completed, the device appears in the Devices list. You can add additional devices by repeating the same steps.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: HP Insights Health Checker - Proxies and Firewalls

items.heading: HP Insights Health Checker - Proxies and Firewalls
Introduction
items.text: The HP Insights Health Checker application has detected that a domain or port is blocked between the PC and HP. This is preventing the PC from enrolling. Please note that, since security is managed in the network where the PC is operating, HP cannot solve this problem. Please read more for the solution.

items.heading: Proxies and Firewalls
items.text: Typically, medium, large, and enterprise businesses use Proxies or Firewalls to protect PCs from resources that might cause harm or from unauthorized access. An approach called whitelisting is commonly used because permits access to approved domains or communication through approved ports. HP Insights requires specific domains and ports be whitelisted or the PC cannot enroll. If the HP Insights Health Checker displayed "HP Endponts unreachable" or "Potential Firewall Issues" please contact the person responsible for your network security.

items.heading: Configuring Domains to Allow Communication for Workforce Experience Platform
Introduction
items.text: Organizations using HP Insights Windows Application (also referred to as Windows Agent) for HP Workforce Experience Platform (WXP) must ensure proper proxy and firewall configurations to ensure secure and uninterrupted communication between devices and cloud services. These configurations enable critical platform functions, such as device enrollment, software updates, telemetry data transmission, and web portal access. This article provides guidance on configuring network settings to support integration for iOS, macOS, Android, and Windows devices. It outlines specific domain and port requirements for AMS and EU data centers and highlights necessary adjustments for various platform components. Key configuration areas include: Users must configure the proxy or firewall to work with the platform for iOS, macOS, Android, and Windows devices. Firewall changes depend on the type of plans and products purchased.

items.heading: SSL inspection software between client and serve
items.text: SSL Inspection tool is used to manage the endpoints. When inspecting SSL traffic with software, users must whitelist the following endpoints to ensure uninterrupted data flow. According to Microsoft's guidance, network devices and services that perform traffic interception, SSL decryption, deep packet inspection, and content filtering should bypass Optimize endpoints to prevent disruptions. This recommendation specifically applies to the following domains This could apply to both fleet-level and device-level settings.

items.heading: IP addresses
items.text: The platform is a cloud-based, Software-as-a-Service application. It uses load balancers to manage incoming traffic to the communication endpoints and can scale up or down based on need. All the URLs are hosted in the cloud with Amazon Web Services (AWS). Communication endpoints are domain-based (For example, hpdaas.com, downloads.hpanalytics.com, etc.) and rely on DNS lookups to route to the correct IP. IP addresses therefore are dynamic, not static. HP does not supply IP addresses for customers to use in whitelists.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: HP Insights Windows Application Does Not Support FIPS

items.heading: HP Insights Windows Application Does Not Support FIPS
Introduction
items.text: FIPS are standards and guidelines for federal computer systems developed by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) per the Federal Information Security Management Act (FISMA) and approved by the Secretary of Commerce. These standards and guidelines are developed when there are no acceptable industry standards or solutions for a particular government requirement. Although FIPS are developed for use by the federal government, many private sectors voluntarily use these standards. For more information, see https://www.nist.gov/standardsgov/compliance-faqs-federal-information-processing-standards-fips.

items.heading: What Happens if FIPS is Enabled?
How to detect if FIPS is Enabled?
items.text: Under the registry key FipsAlgorithmPolicy, if the Enabled value is set to 1, then FIPS is enabled. Registry key path: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\FIPSAlgorithmPolicy

items.heading: Installing HP Insights Windows Application Using Setup Method
Introduction
items.text: The HP Insights Windows Application (also called Windows Agent) can be installed via setup method, which ensures that all necessary components, dependencies, and configurations are properly set up to support the agent. The installation process includes the HP Insights package, HP Insights Analytics agent, and associated dependencies. This helps to ensure seamless data collection and reporting. This article provides step-by-step guide to: The setup consists of the following separate installation packages, with each package having its own independent version, prerequisites (as visible in the Add or Remove programs on a Windows device), and a Windows Task Scheduler task.

items.heading: Installing Windows Agent Using the Setup Method

items.heading: Installing HP Insights Windows Application Using MSI Installer
Introduction
items.text: The HP Insights Windows Application (also called Windows Agent) for HP Workforce Experience Platform (WXP) can also be installed using the MSI installer, which is suitable for scripted or enterprise-wide deployments through tools like Microsoft Endpoint Configuration Manager. The MSI package includes the HP Insights Windows Application, but does not automatically install additional components, such as the HP Insights Analytics Agent or supporting dependencies. These must be installed separately to enable complete data collection and reporting. This article outlines the steps to deploy the Windows Agent for WXP using an MSI Installer. Note: Do not install the MSI version on devices that are already enrolled using the setup installer.

The VC++ Redistributable applications are required for the deployment of the agent. However, some devices might have an updated version of these applications and will be suitable for deployment. The Manager Application will also require the Company PIN (also referred to as CPIN). To obtain the PIN,

items.heading: Post-installation details
items.text: Once installed, the Windows Agent will populate the data in the devices list with HW/SW inventory. This is in semi-real time which checks every 15 minutes to send updates. If the Agent is uninstalled from the device, the Agent will also attempt to unenroll from the server side (if it has internet). The devices page will be updated immediately once it sees the unenroll alert. The server will keep the device in the list but mark it as Unenrolled. The HP Insights Analytics Agent will pull info (much more than the Agent) and upload it to the server once a day after installation. The server has a daily job to process the data and populate the dashboard and charts. Hence there is a delay between the dashboard/charts vs. the devices list. Note: It is not possible to install the HP Insights Analytics Agent - Dependencies package on non-HP manufactured devices, including Virtual Machines. It enables private API calls to get telemetry from the HP HW component.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Overview of Automatic Enrollment of Devices

items.heading: Overview of Automatic Enrollment of Devices
Introduction
items.text: Automatic enrollment is a key feature of the HP Workforce Experience Platform (WXP) that simplifies and secures device onboarding at scale. It enables you (IT teams) to register, configure, and manage devices without manual intervention. This approach is ideal for seamless deployment across hybrid, remote, and enterprise environments.

items.heading: Target Audience
items.text: Primary audience: Secondary audience:

items.heading: Key Features
items.text: Some of the key features are as follows:

items.heading: Popular Use Cases
items.text: Some of the popular use cases of automatic enrollment as are:

items.heading: Automatic Enrollment - Windows
items.text: For Windows devices, automatic enrollment involves enrolling devices to manage settings, enforce security policies, deploy applications, and provide updates across a fleet of devices. The automatic aspect removes the need for manual, device-by-device setup, which is cumbersome for large-scale deployments. HP Workforce Experience Platform offers the following options to automatically enroll Windows devices. Method Description Usage Company PIN (setup.exe) Uses a Company PIN and the setup.exe installer to register devices during installation. This lightweight method allows devices to be manually or programmatically enrolled during the initial software installation. Ideal when you want a quick way to enroll devices during installation using a unique PIN, particularly in small deployments or when distributing devices outside of IT control. Install.CMD Batch Script Automates enrollment using a batch script (Install.CMD) that invokes setup.exe during system startup. The script checks if the agent is installed and initiates enrollment. Suitable for managing devices via a local network and need to automate enrollment at boot through scripts. Ideal for GPO-managed environments with limited internet access. Microsoft Active Directory Group Policy (GPO) Leverages Group Policy Objects (GPO) in an on-premises Active Directory environment to push the Install.CMD script to all domain-joined devices for enrollment. Ideal when you want a centralized control in a traditional enterprise environment and need to automatically deploy the agent to domain-joined Windows machines. Microsoft Intune (MSI Installer) Enables automatic enrollment through Microsoft Intune by packaging the MSI version of the agent and deploying it via endpoint configuration profiles. Recommended for cloud-based environments, especially where remote device management is required. This approach is beneficial for organizations using Microsoft Intune for device management, as it facilitates deployment across diverse geographical locations. Microsoft Intune (setup.exe Installer) Deploys the setup.exe version of the installer via Microsoft Intune, providing greater flexibility during installation. Suitable when you prefer using setup.exe for its additional options or silent install parameters, while still leveraging Intune's cloud-based deployment capabilities. Microsoft System Center Configuration Manager (SCCM - MSI Installer) Installs and enrolls the WXP agent using SCCM and the MSI installer. Offers silent deployment, package control, and status monitoring for IT administrators. Best suited for environments with SCCM infrastructure for complex environments that need advanced deployment control and monitoring. Microsoft System Center Configuration Manager (SCCM - setup.exe Installer) Enables enrollment through SCCM using the setup.exe version of the installer, offering additional customization options. Ideal when you need the configuration flexibility of setup.exe combined with the centralized deployment capabilities of SCCM. Ivanti Integrates WXP agent deployment with Ivanti endpoint management tools. Ivanti can be used to deploy the installation files and trigger enrollment silently across a managed fleet. Ideal when already using Ivanti for software deployment or asset management and want to embed enrollment into your existing device management workflows.
