https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Tech Insights Analytics Data Processing Schedule

items.heading: Tech Insights Analytics Data Processing Schedule
Introduction
items.text: HP WXP Collaboration application processes data for Tech Insights Analytics nightly, Sunday through Thursday, with a weekly maintenance window on Saturday. Processing is based on Central Time (North America). As a result, Tech Insights Analytics reflects data from your environment as of the previous business day. It can take up to 24 to 48 hours for endpoints to appear in the Tech Insights Monitoring and Tech Insights Analytics modules. Endpoint and location tagging for Tech Insights Analytics is usually available the next business day, but in some cases it may take 24 to 48 hours before it can be used in groups and filters. Note: WXP Collaboration operates using standard time and does not apply daylight savings time adjustments. All reported times are shown without daylight savings offsets. If data is not visible for a piece of infrastructure, you may be prompted to confirm that you want the piece infrastructure enabled. Select OK to enable it. After data collection begins for a device, it may take up to 48 hours for data to appear in Tech Insights Analytics. This delay occurs because historical data is processed once per day during overnight processing. Note: Tech Insights Monitoring and Tech Insights Analytics data may not always match. Tech Insights Monitoring displays near real-time data collected directly from endpoints and infrastructure. Tech Insights Analytics processes historical data, such as call records and system configuration, during nightly processing. Note: Tech Insights Monitoring and Tech Insights Analytics data may not always match.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Understanding Monitors vs Events Based Monitoring

items.heading: Understanding Monitors vs Events Based Monitoring
Introduction
items.text: WXP Collaboration meticulously gathers a substantial volume of data points from interconnected systems. With this wealth of data, WXP Collaboration offers two distinct monitoring solutions to its customers. This article elucidates these two monitoring solutions and their respective applications.

items.heading: Metrics Based Monitoring
items.text: Metrics-based monitoring represents the prevailing method with which our customers are most acquainted. This approach entails the ceaseless acquisition and examination of quantitative data emanating from diverse facets of a system. These data points are inherently numerical, affording a profound understanding of a system's or call's performance, health, and behavioral patterns over time. Metrics based monitoring excels in evaluating call quality and the volume of calls directed towards a system. However, it may not always be the optimal choice for ascertaining system availability or peripheral status. This is where Event-Based Monitoring steps in, presenting a realm of new possibilities for alerting, a capability that WXP Collaboration was previously unable to offer.

items.heading: Event Based Monitoring
items.text: In stark contrast, Event Based Monitoring is centered on the occurrence of precise events or incidents within a system. This mode of monitoring proves especially effective in promptly detecting and responding to incidents, errors, and other pivotal events. Unlike metrics-based systems, event-based systems operate on defined rules or patterns to trigger alerts. There are many benefits to Event Based Monitoring. It facilitates the monitoring of system availability, peripheral status (coming soon), and, in the case of Cisco endpoints, diagnostics messages. The latter, in particular, is of notable significance, as Cisco endpoints afford access to a staggering array of 194 distinct event types, each accompanied by various message types. Initially, WXP Collaboration concentrated on Cisco endpoints due to the profusion of event types that can be gathered and leveraged for alerts. Nevertheless, we are steadfastly laboring towards incorporating Events from diverse device manufacturers. Creating an Event Monitor for how to use Event Based Monitoring

items.heading: Contact Us
items.text: For any assistance, create a support case or email support@wxp.hp.com.

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: This article will walk through creating an Event monitor within WXP Collaboration. To read the difference between Events and Metrics, please click here.

items.heading: Creating An Event
items.text: If your organization is enabled for creating Events, you can click on the Add Monitor button, which opens up a dropdown menu. In the dropdown, you can select "Event Monitor".

Now that we're on the Event Monitor Page, let's go over the "Events" section under the "Set filter conditions". There are six different attributes you can choose from. Three of them are visible when you open a new events page: Origin type is used for the type of system you want to accept events for. Currently, the only option for the Event type is "Endpoints". We will be adding other origin types, like System, in the future. The Event Type is what Event you want to get alerted on. There are over 190 different Events to choose from. "Endpoint Down" and "Endpoint In Call" are WXP Collaboration-generated Events, while the rest of the Events are events that Cisco Endpoints have the capability of sending as a Diagnostic Message. WXP Collaboration does not know the type of messages that Cisco Endpoints can/will send. The Level is the level of the event coming from the source system. For Example, if you choose "Camera Pairing" as an event type. The endpoint can have different messages for the error, and with each error, it can have a different event level. So camera pairing event types can either come in as Critical or Warning, for instance. The Level option in WXP Collaboration allows you to either accept any level and get alerted on that, or you can filter down to get only Critical Level Events from the endpoint itself. Message allows you to filter on what messages you want to get alerted on, based on what the system is sending. It has the normal operators that the WXP Collaboration generally provides. It's recommended that if you do not know what messages are coming in, you do not use this filter until you have determined how you want to filter your events. Category currently holds two values. Endpoint Status and Cisco Diagnostics. This is an added filter to narrow down whether you just want to receive Endpoint Status Events or Cisco Diagnostics. The Origin ID is the identifier for the origin object. Currently, this is related to the endpoint you want to select. However, it is recommended you use the Origin Type (e.g, Endpoints) filter. The second section under "Set filter conditions" allows you to filter what endpoints you would like the monitor to keep monitoring. You have the ability to select certain endpoints, use tags, or any other available field in the menu. It's recommended to use tags; that way, as a user, you do not have to constantly add endpoints.

items.heading: Defining a status trigger
items.text: The Define a status trigger allows you to select a status for your events that is not the same as what the endpoint sends for your emails/webhooks and set the duration of how long the event needs to be available before it is triggered as a notification.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Investigating Mismatches Between Tech Insights Analytics and Rolled Back Tech Insights Monitoring

items.heading: Investigating Mismatches Between Tech Insights Analytics and Rolled Back Tech Insights Monitoring
Introduction
items.text: This article explains why Tech Insights Monitoring and Tech Insights Analytics can display different call data, based on their distinct data sources, collection methods, and processing logic.

items.heading: Causes for Data Mismatch
items.text: Tech Insights Monitoring and Tech Insights Analytics use different data sources, so the data may not match. Tech Insights Monitoring takes real-time data feeds from endpoints and infrastructure and reflects them in the Tech Insights Monitoring module. Tech Insights Analytics takes historical data, such as call records and system configuration, and processes these for display in the Tech Insights Analytics module. There is potential for a mismatch if either Tech Insights Monitoring or Tech Insights Analytics does not receive all relevant data. For example, consider a CUCM for which FTP transfers to the data collector are blocked at the network level. In this case, Tech Insights Monitoring may 'see' the calls via the CUCM's JTAPI interface, but Tech Insights Analytics would not receive any call records, and therefore would not display any analytical data for those calls. Similarly, if it were not possible to contact the Call Manager service on the CUCM, but the CDR records were successfully delivered, then Monitoring may not show a call that Analytics does. In general, both real-time and call record information must be available to ensure that the calls displayed in Monitoring match those in Analytics. There remains one further cause of apparent mismatch. Because of the nature of the real-time data, it is possible that individual call legs associated with a call may display separately. Since the data displayed in Tech Insights Analytics has been subject to considerable processing, call legs are often merged in this display when they represent redundant data.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Identifying What Causes WXP Collaboration to Report a System as Down

items.heading: Identifying What Causes WXP Collaboration to Report a System as Down
Introduction
items.text: The WXP Collaboration platform provides comprehensive system-status monitoring to ensure real-time visibility into your environment, leveraging data collectors and integrated APIs to accurately identify and report connectivity disruptions.

items.heading: Causes for WXP Collaboration to Report a System as Down
items.text: If a system cannot be reached by the WXP Collaboration data collector, then it will be considered 'down'. In most cases, the data collector will contact a system via the vendor-provided API, and, if a response is received, the system will be considered to be "up." However, if the attempt to connect to the API fails, then the system will be flagged down. It should be noted that the status of the system is the data collector's view - if environmental issues, such as network problems, proxy issues, firewall rule changes, etc., prevent collection, then the WXP Collaboration platform has no option other than marking the system as down. Some systems are contacted via a database connection - if the credentials are incorrect (for instance, a password has expired), then this may also cause a system to be marked as down since the data collector will be unable to connect to the database. In order to detect when your data collector is down, you can use the WXP Collaboration Tech Insights Monitoring API. When using the API Systems call, one is returned a list of systems that are monitored by WXP Collaboration. These systems fall into two categories: on-premise systems that the data collector gathers data from, and cloud systems (Webex, Zoom, BlueJeans) that may be collected on your behalf by WXP Collaboration. If all local systems are flagged as 'unavailable,' then it may be inferred that either the collector is down or there is a catastrophic local networking failure. Conversely, if the only systems that are marked as OK are cloud systems, the same inference can be made. In order to achieve this with the API, query https://api.vyopta.com/cpmm/api/v1/orgs/{{orgID}}/systems with a filter of ``` filter=type!=BJN;type!=ZOOM;type!=CISCOWEBEX;status=="Unreachable" ```. If no systems are returned, assume the collector is down. You can also use WXP Collaboration's new Intelligent Monitoring and Alerts to create custom Collector Down Alerts. In this example, we have used a call metric to alert us when the WXP Collaboration Data Collector

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Writing a Custom Monitor Webhook: Syntax and Variables

items.heading: Writing a Custom Monitor Webhook: Syntax and Variables
Introduction
items.text: The purpose of this knowledge base article is to help guide you through Webhooks that were introduced within our Tech Insights Monitoring - Monitors Notification. Webhooks allow you, as a user, to send triggered monitor notifications directly to any publicly accessible system to generate tickets, create chat alerts, or run automation!

items.heading: Understanding the Syntax
items.text: Webhooks under Tech Insights Monitoring Monitors follow the JSON standard; however, to allow customization, a few basic functions were created that can be inserted into the JSON document. Which we will walk through below: Provided Syntax is: Quick Examples If Statement [# th:if="${VARIABLE}" ] Inline If Else [(${VARIABLE} ?: 'Not specified')] System Variables {[(${alertId})] Unless [# th:unless="${VARIABLE} == 0" ]

items.heading: Syntax Walkthrough
items.text: System Variables: System variables are WXP Collaboration cloud variables used to populate data about the alert. This is used to provide data about the alert when it's sent out to your system/messaging app. Variable Name Variable ID Description Example Category [(${category})] Provides the category selected when the monitor was created. Endpoints Clear Duration (minutes) [(${okDurationMinutes})] When a Clear status is sent, this will indicate in minutes how long the issue has been in that state 10 Consecutive Minutes [(${alertDurationMinutes})] The Duration (in minutes) of the issue when the monitor was triggered 30 Created By Email [(${createdByEmail})] Email of the user that created the monitor john.doe@abc*.com Created By Name [(${createdByName})] Name of the user that created the monitor John Doe Created Time (milliseconds) [(${createdTimeMS})] The time the monitor was created in Epoch Time 636741066307 Critical Threshold [(${errorThreshold})] The critical threshold number set under Conditions in Monitors 100 [(${description})] Provides a description of the monitor configuration Endpoint in ( System1 ) Fired Time (milliseconds) [(${alertFiredTimeMs})] This is the Time the Monitor was Triggered. This is provided in Epoch Time 1636743066312 ID [(${alertId})] This provides the Alert ID b5867e97-0952-4fbe-95ea-0d313170223e Info Threshold [(${infoThreshold})] The info threshold number set under Conditions in Monitors Item Count [(${deviceCount})] Provides a count of the selected items in the monitor Metric [(${metric})] Provides the Metric selected when the monitor was created. In Call Count Modified By Email [(${modifiedByEmail})] jane.doe@abc*.com Modified By Name [(${modifiedByName})] Name of the user that last modified the monitor Jane Doe Modified Time (milliseconds) [(${modifiedTimeMs})] The time the monitor was last modified in Epoch Time 636743066307 Name [(${name})] Alert Name which is set when creating the Monitor (Monitor Name) Test Monitor Operation [(${operation})] This is the "Triggered when metric is" which is in the "Set alert conditions" on the monitors page.The options are:Greater Than or EqualsGreater ThanLess ThanLess Than or EqualsEqualsNot Equals This is the "Triggered when metric is" which is in the "Set alert conditions" on the monitors page. The options are: equal to Selected Items [(${selectedItems})] Provides the names of the selected items in the monitor. Comma Delimited System1, System2 Status [(${status})] Provides the Status of the Monitor. Either CRITICAL, WARN, INFO CRITICAL Status Color (hex) [(${statusColor})] Provides the HEX for the color of the type of Status. #DD4B39 Status Start Time (milliseconds) [(${statusStartTimeMs})] When the monitor has changed to that status in Epoch Time Threshold [(${threshold})] The threshold that was triggered for either Critical, Warn and Info Url [(${alertUrl})] A URL that takes your directly to the monitor that has been triggered with the timestamp marker as a vertical line Warn Threshold [(${warnThreshold})] The warn threshold number set under Conditions in Monitors 50 Window (hours) [(${lookbackDurationHours})] The "Over the last" option under conditions - Generally not provided unless using Call Records. 4

items.heading: Functions in Body:
items.text: To allow flexibility, Functions were introduced in the Webooks section. This allows the user to tweak what data should be sent.

items.heading: If Statement:
items.text: There may be a time when you want to use an if statement. Below is an example of displaying "Critical Threshold is X" if the "errorThreshold" variable is being sent [# th:if="${errorThreshold}" ], { "Critical Threshold is [(${errorThreshold})]" }[/]

items.heading: Unless Statement:
items.text: The unless statement is as simple as the if statement mentioned above. Some variables will always exist; however, provide a number of 0. In the example below, we are going to display a message unless "Clear Duration Minutes" is equal to 0. [# th:unless="${clearDurationMinutes} == 0" ], { "[(${clearDurationMinutes})] minutes" }[/]

items.heading: Inline If Else:
items.text: Inline If Else can be used to quickly check if a variable exists and replace it with a message of your own. [(${criticalThreshold} ?: 'Not specified')] An example of the inline If statement to check if the "Critical Threshold" was specified and if not, replace it with "Not specified" can be done like this: Webhook Headers Webhooks also allow headers to be configured in the body of the webhook. When a header is needed, it needs to be entered before the JSON and with a line break in between. Multiple headers can be set to be passed onto the remote server. Example headers are: ● Content-Type ● Authorization Below is an example of using the Content-Type and Authorization Headers to send a Webex message: Content-Type: application/json Authorization: Bearer <Token> { "roomId": <Webex RoomId>, "markdown": "This will be sent as a message" }

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: When validating an infrastructure component or endpoint in the HP WXP Collaboration Admin Portal, the validation fails and a 504 error message is displayed. A 504 error indicates that a gateway timeout occurred. In this scenario, the gateway is the WXP Collaboration Data Collector. The error occurs when the validation request cannot be completed within the expected time.
This article provides guidance to resolve a Validation Failed error with a 504 status code when validating infrastructure or endpoints in the WXP Collaboration Admin Portal.

items.heading: Resolution
items.text: The 504 error can occur in the following scenarios.

items.heading: Scenario 1: Broker Connection Is Not Enabled
items.text: With the release of the WXP Collaboration Data Collector, infrastructure and endpoint configuration moved to the cloud-based WXP Collaboration Admin Portal. Validation requests are processed through a broker connection between the portal and the collector. If the broker connection is not enabled for your organization, validation requests cannot be completed. This is indicated by a red infinity symbol with a line through it in the portal.

To enable this capability, contact the owner of your WXP Collaboration installation within your organization. If your organization approves enabling the broker interface, contact WXP Collaboration Support for assistance.

items.heading: Scenario 2: Underpowered Collector Server
items.text: A 504 error can also occur if the server hosting the WXP Collaboration Data Collector is underpowered. In this case, the collector cannot process validation requests before the portal times out. The collector server should maintain a maximum CPU utilization of 70 percent to allow sufficient overhead for processing spikes.

items.heading: Minimum Collector Server Requirements
items.text: Ensure that the collector server meets the minimum requirements listed below.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Debugging Infrastructure and Endpoint Connectivity Issues Using PortQryUI

items.heading: Debugging Infrastructure and Endpoint Connectivity Issues Using PortQryUI
Introduction
items.text: Connectivity issues between the HP WXP Collaboration Data Collection agent and infrastructure or endpoints are among the most common challenges faced by local WXP Collaboration administrators. These issues are often diagnosed using a web browser, Telnet, or PuTTY. While these tools are commonly used, they have several limitations: This article guides you to download, Install, and Use PortQryUI, a Windows Servce network debuggin ulitity.

items.heading: Overview of PortQryUI
items.text: Earlier versions of Windows Server included a network debugging utility called PortQry. Although PortQry is no longer bundled with Windows Server, it is still available for download. A graphical version of the tool, PortQryUI, is also available. This article focuses on PortQryUI, though the command-line version is included and may be used if preferred. You can download PortQryUI from the following Microsoft site:https://www.microsoft.com/en-gb/download/details.aspx?id=24009

items.heading: Installation
items.text: PortQryUI is distributed as a self-extracting ZIP archive (PortQryUI.exe) and does not require a formal installation.
Once extraction is completed, the tool is ready for use.

items.heading: Using PortQryUI
items.text: To launch PortQryUI:
For example, to check connectivity to a CUCM API hosted at 192.168.2.20 on port 8443: The output shows:
Understanding PortQry Status Messages The output may be interpreted as follows: Microsoft defines the following PortQry status values:

items.heading: Defining services to use with the Query predefined serviceoption.
items.text: If there are queries that are often used these may be specified in the config.xml file that is present in the same folder as the executables. For more information, see the config.xml and PortQueryUI.doc files.

items.heading: Fine-grained Control Using the Command Line Tool
items.text: If it is necessary to change the default behaviour, for instance to set a custom SNMP community string, the PortQry executable may be run in a command window. When running in this manner, there is a convenient interactive mode that gives the fullest control over the tool - this may be used by running PortQry -i PS C:\PortQryUI> ./portqry -i PortQry Interactive Mode Type 'help' for a list of commands Default Node: 127.0.0.1 Current option values: end port= 80 protocol= TCP source port= 0 (ephemeral) Entering a ? will provide helpful informationPS C:\PortQryUI> ./portqry -i PortQry Interactive Mode Type 'help' for a list of commands Default Node: 127.0.0.1 Current option values: end port= 80 protocol= TCP source port= 0 (ephemeral) > ? Valid Commands: =============== help or ? - display info on common commands phelp or ?p - display list of frequently used ports node NAME - set default node to query, NAME or IP address query or q - send query to default node set OPTION=value Options: all - display current option values port=n - set port number to query - set port= or set e= sport=n - set source port number, 0=ephemeral - set sport= or set sp= protocol=p - set protocol used for query, TCP, UDP, or BOTH - set protocol= or set p= cn=string - set SNMP community name - default is set to public nr - toggles reverse name lookups - enables/disables resolving node IP address to name sl - toggles slow link delay for UDP queries - doubles timeout period waiting for UDP responses query shortcut... Therefore, to query SNMP on a system using a different community string, do the following: PS C:\PortQryUI> ./portqry -i PortQry Interactive Mode Type 'help' for a list of commands Default Node: 127.0.0.1 Current option values: end port= 80 protocol= TCP source port= 0 (ephemeral) > node 192.168.2.20 Default Node: 192.168.2.20 > > set cn=myCommunityString SNMP community name set to: myCommunityString Reminder: community names are case sensitive > q snmp resolving service name using local services file... UDP port resolved to the 'snmp' service IP address resolved to ip-192-168-2-20.ec2.internal querying... UDP port 161 (snmp service): LISTENING or FILTERED In order to get a response from the SNMP service, the community name in the query must match a community name that the SNMP service is configured to accept By default PortQry uses the community name: public Use the 'set cn' command to specify a different name if needed community name for query: myCommunityString Sending SNMP query to UDP port 161... UDP port 161 (snmp service): FILTERED Removing PortQryUI To remove PortQryUI, delete the folder containing the extracted files. The tool is not installed as a Windows application and leaves no system-level components behind.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Understanding the Object Error During WXP Collaboration Login

items.heading: Understanding the Object Error During WXP Collaboration Login
items.text: When attempting to sign in to HP WXP Collaboration, some users may encounter an error message displayed as “[object Object]”. This message indicates a login failure related to platform access rather than an issue with user credentials.

items.heading: Cause
items.text: This error typically occurs when a user attempts to access the WXP Collaboration Tech Insights platform (vanalytics.vyopta.com) before completing the required enablement steps.

items.heading: Resolution
items.text: If you are logging in to complete the Pre-Enablement checklist, ensure that you access WXP Collaboration through the WXP Collaboration Admin Portal instead of the Tech Insights platform. If you have already completed the enablement process and continue to see this error, submit a support request through the WXP Collaboration Online Support Request Form so that the issue can be investigated.

items.text: For any assistance, create a support case or email support@wxp.hp.com.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: IP Addresses for WXP Collaboration Applications

items.heading: IP Addresses for WXP Collaboration Applications
Introduction
items.text: HP WXP Collaboration application is a modern Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) application deployed in a cloud computing environment. Due to the nature of cloud infrastructure, WXP Collaboration does not support or guarantee static IPv4 addresses.

items.heading: Cloud Infrastructure Considerations
items.text: In cloud environments, IP addresses can change dynamically. Customers using legacy firewall or network infrastructure, that requires IP addresses for external access, are responsible performing regular checking of WXP CollaborationHost Name to IP address translation using a DNS query tool, for instance nslookup or dig, and keep their infrastructure updated accordingly.

items.heading: WXP Collaboration Host Names
items.text: Customers who require IP addresses for network access should use the following WXP Collaboration host names for DNS resolution: Regular monitoring and updates are required to ensure firewall rules remain aligned with the current IP addresses associated with these host names.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Customer Setup for Acme Packet or Oracle SBC Collector

items.heading: Customer Setup for Acme Packet or Oracle SBC Collector
Introduction
items.text: This article describes the Oracle-specific configuration required for HP WXP Collaboration to collect data from Oracle Session Border Controllers (SBC) and EOM / OCOM systems. It covers required CLI access, CDR configuration, FTP forwarding, and REST API enablement.

items.heading: SSH ACLI Access
items.text: WXP Collaboration collects Oracle SBC data using the SSH Acme Command Line Interface (ACLI). A single SSH ACLI user account with standard user privileges is required. Superuser access is not required. The SSH user account must have sufficient privileges to run the following commands: Verify access by running these commands in a terminal session using the assigned user account. For more information about ACLI, refer to the Oracle ACLI Reference Guide.

items.heading: Enable the cdr-output-inclusive Setting
items.text: The cdr-output-inclusive setting must be enabled before deploying the WXP Collaboration data collector for Oracle SBC. This setting ensures a consistent number of columns for each CDR record type, making interpretation of CDR data possible. CDR files do not include headers. To enable this setting in the Oracle SBC web interface:

items.heading: Enabling Forwarding of Generated CDR Files over FTP
items.text: This configuration requires a standalone FTP server running on the same system as the WXP Collaboration Data Collector. An example SFTP setup using FreeFTPd is available. Follow steps 1 through 3 only. The Oracle SBC historical collector relies upon the Oracle SBC functionality of pushing CDRs as they are generated to another server (in this case, the on-prem WXP Collaboraton Data Collector server) over FTP. This can be configured through the Oracle SBC web GUI:

Use the FTP credentials configured on the FTP server running on the WXP Collaboration Data Collector system. FTP forwarding can also be configured using the superuser ACLI interface. For details, refer to the Oracle Accounting Guide.

items.heading: Enable FTP Access
items.text: WXP Collaboration historical collectors retrieve CDR and MDR files from EOM / OCOM systems using FTP.

items.heading: Enable the EOM / OCOM REST API
items.text: WXP Collaboration real-time collectors use the EOM / OCOM REST API to collect call and voice quality data.
Note: WXP Collaboration data collector uses the following REST API functions:getPagedCallsgetCallDetailscallVoiceQuality Note: WXP Collaboration data collector uses the following REST API functions:

items.heading: Adding a New User shows User Already Exists error
Introduction
items.text: When adding a new user in HP WXP Collaboration application, you may receive an error stating that the user already exists, even though the user does not appear in the active Users list. This article explains why this error occurs and how to resolve it.

items.heading: Cause
items.text: This error occurs when the user was previously added to the account and later deleted or removed. In this case, the user record still exists in the Recycle Bin and cannot be added again until it is restored.
Note: Only users with access to the Admin Portal can restore users from the Recycle Bin.For more information about user management, refer to the article Managing Users. Note:

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: In Tech Insights Monitoring of HP , an Endpoint Connectivity status of "NoMessages" and an EPM Error message of "Updated by Server" indicate the same condition.

These messages indicate that the Endpoint Monitor (EPM) has not received any data from the endpoint during the last 10 minutes. This indicates a loss of connectivity between the endpoint and the Endpoint Monitor. When either or both messages appear, you should treat them as a connectivity issue between the endpoint and the Endpoint Monitor. You should begin troubleshooting the issue from the Admin Portal of the WXP Collaboration application

In addition to verifying credentials, try to access the endpoint web interface using a web browser (or curl on Linux systems). Note: Use the same credentials stored in the Admin Portal of WXP Collaboration application to confirm that you can successfully log in to the endpoint.

Introduction
items.text: You may encounter an error message from a Cisco codec- "The HTTP Feedback for slot X cannot be delivered.” This issue is commonly related to certificate validation or an outdated HP WXP Collaboration Data Collector. You may see one or more of the following error messages:
A reboot of the endpoint may be required after unregistering the feedback slot. Refer to the Knowledge Base for additional information.

items.heading: Disable Certificate Verification (If Required)
items.text: If you continue to see SSL certificate warnings, you can disable certificate verification by running the following command on the Cisco endpoint: xconfiguration HttpFeedback TlsVerify: Off After applying this configuration, confirm that HTTP feedback errors are no longer reported.

items.text: Occasionally, in HP WXP Collaboration application, you may need to stop Tech Insights Monitoring from collecting data from a specific system. When data collection is disabled, Tech Insights Monitoring stops collecting data from the system. https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Infrastructure Status is Showing Unreachable in Tech Insights Monitoring

items.heading: Infrastructure Status is Showing Unreachable in Tech Insights Monitoring
Introduction
items.text: Some infrastructure devices may appear with a status of Unreachable in Tech Insights Monitoring of HP WXP Collaboration application even though data collection is functioning correctly.

items.heading: Understanding Unreachable Status in Monitoring
items.text: Tech Insights Monitoring reflects the ability to collect real-time call data from an infrastructure device. Certain platforms, such as Microsoft Teams, do not support real-time data collection through their available APIs. As a result, these platforms may appear as Unreachable in the Monitoring view.

items.heading: Understanding Impact on Analytics Data
items.text: The Unreachable status does not affect data used by Tech Insights Analytics. Analytics data is collected through different mechanisms and remains available even when a device appears as Unreachable in Tech Insights Monitoring.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Creating a Room Distribution List for Room Sync

items.heading: Creating a Room Distribution List for Room Sync
Introduction
items.text: HP WXP Collaboration supports the creation of Room Distribution Lists for the Workspace Insights feature to accurately identify and group room mailboxes from Exchange. This configuration is required for collecting and reporting meeting room availability, usage, and utilization data. Proper configuration of Room Distribution Lists ensures that room data is discovered and displayed correctly in Workspace Insights dashboards and analytics. This article describes the steps required to create Room Distribution Lists (RDLs) for WXP Collaboration > Workspace Insights. Note: Room Distribution Lists are different from Office 365 (O365) Groups. O365 Groups are not supported and do not work for room synchronization.

items.heading: Prerequisites
items.text: The following prerequisites are required to create Room Distribution Lists:

items.heading: Creating Room Distribution
items.text: 1. Connect to the Exchange Online service by running the following PowerShell command: Connect-ExchangeOnline -UserPrincipalName <Email Address>

New-DistributionGroup -Name "Vyopta Rooms" -PrimarySmtpAddress "VyoptaRooms@example.com" -RoomList Add-DistributionGroupMember -Identity "Vyopta Rooms" -Member "Room1" To add all room mailboxes, run the following PowerShell commands: $Members=Get-Mailbox -Filter {(RecipientTypeDetails -eq "RoomMailbox")} foreach ($member in $Members) { Add-DistributionGroupMember -Identity "Vyopta Rooms" -Member $member.Alias } 4. Verify that room mailboxes are associated with the Room Distribution List by running the following PowerShell command: Get-DistributionGroupMember -Identity "Vyopta Rooms" | ft Name, PrimarySMTPAddress -AutoSize

items.heading: Converting a Distribution List to a Room Distribution List
items.text: An existing Distribution List can be converted to a Room Distribution List for Workspace Insights. Note: The distribution list must contain only room mailboxes. After verifying that the distribution list does not include user mailboxes, run the following PowerShell command: Set-DistributionGroup -Identity "Vyopta Rooms" -RoomList

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: Cisco Meeting Server (CMS) provisions new user accounts with a default password aging policy. By default, passwords expire after 180 days. As a result, service accounts used for integrations, such as the WXP Collaboration service account (vyopta_svc), must have their passwords updated twice each year. If the password for the service account expires, WXP Collaboration Data Collectors cannot collect data from CMS, and Call Detail Records (CDRs) may be lost. CMS (formerly Acano) does not provide for onboard persistent storage for past CDR events. To avoid interruptions in data collection, administrators must extend the password expiration duration in CMS.

items.heading: Extending Default Expiration of Password
items.text: If your corporate service account policies allow it, extend the default password expiration duration before creating the WXP Collaboration service account. user rule password_age NNNN user rule password_age 1825 This command updates the default password aging rule for accounts that will be newly created.

items.heading: Extending the Expiration for an Existing Service Account
items.text: If the vyopta_svc account has already been created, extend the password duration using the following steps. passwd vyopta_svc The password does not need to change. Re-entering the same password refreshes the expiration duration. Note: If you choose to change the password instead of re-entering the existing password, you must also:Update the password in the Admin Portal of WXP Collaboration.Validate the infrastructure to ensure data collection continues without interruption. Note: If you choose to change the password instead of re-entering the existing password, you must also:

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Supporting People Count Sensors in Space Insights module of WXP Collaboration

items.heading: Supporting People Count Sensors in Space Insights module of WXP Collaboration
Introduction
items.text: Space Insights in WXP Collaboration supports ingesting data from third-party people count sensors. This capability helps organizations collect room utilization data for workspaces that do not have video conferencing endpoints, such as focus rooms and small huddle spaces, thereby extending occupancy tracking to more workspaces in the organization. After a sensor is mapped to a room, the sensor data contributes to the utilization metrics for that room in Space Insights. Currently, the supported sensor vendor is Kontakt.io.

items.heading: Adding Sensors to WXP Collaboration
items.text: To use people count sensors with Space Insights, an administrator must first add and authorize a integration with the deployed sensor platform such as Kontakt on the Infrastructure page. After the sensor has been integrated , the admin portal displays a Sensors page that lists the sensor devices deployed in the customer sensor platform.

items.heading: Mapping Sensors to Rooms
items.text: The administrator must then manually map each sensor to the appropriate room in Space Insights. To map sensors to rooms, the follow these steps: Once sensors are mapped to rooms, people count data from the sensors will populate applicable metrics for those rooms in Space Insights.

items.heading: Updating an Existing Mapping
items.text: If a sensor is moved to a different room, the mapping must be updated in the Sensors CSV file and uploaded again. To update a mapping: The system preserves mapping history based on the timestamp of the mapping change. Note:A sensor can be mapped to only one room at a time.Sensor data populates metrics only from the time of mapping onward.Historical data is not backfilled for time periods before the mapping was created.The mapping history between sensors and rooms is preserved over time.Sensors are commonly used for rooms without endpoints, but they can also be used for rooms with endpoints if people count is not otherwise available. Note:

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: The WXP Collaboration Data Collector Support Lifecycle

items.heading: The WXP Collaboration Data Collector Support Lifecycle
Introduction
items.text: In order to ensure that we are able to offer the latest features to our customers, it is necessary that the Vyopta Data Collector be upgraded when it has reached the end of its supported lifetime.

items.heading: Policy
items.text: Vyopta has adopted a policy of supporting data collector release of the current GA (General Availability) release and two (2) previous GA releases. For example, if the current GA release is at version 4.9.1, then both version 4.8.8 and 4.8.7 will also be supported - older releases will not be supported. Once a collector has had a GA release, there will be a 30-day grace period for performing any necessary upgrades of collectors that have newly reached end of support. When a data collector has reached the end of its supported lifetime (as defined above), Vyopta will be unable to assist customers with collection issues until such time as the collector is upgraded to a supported version. Version End of Support Vyopta Tech Insights Data Collector 4.10.2 TBD (30 days following release of 4.11.0 RC or equivalent GA) Vyopta Tech Insights Data Collector 4.9.4 RC TBD (30 days following release of 4.9.6 RC or equivalent GA) Vyopta Tech Insights Data Collector 4.9.2 18th May 2025 Vyopta Tech Insights Data Collector 4.9.1 23rd October 2024 Vyopta Tech Insights Data Collector 4.8.8 16th March 2024 Vyopta Tech Insights Data Collector 4.8.7 13th March 2024 Vyopta Tech Insights Data Collector 4.8.6 2nd August 2023 Vyopta Tech Insights Data Collector 4.8.5 1st March 2023 Vyopta Tech Insights Data Collector 4.8.4 6th February 2023 Vyopta Tech Insights Data Collector 4.8.3 or earlier 22nd October 2022 Vyopta Tech Insights Data Collector 4.7 or earlier 17th January 2022 vAnalytics Data Collector 3.x or earlier 30th September 2021 Vyopta Endpoint Monitor 3.x or earlier

items.heading: Vyopta Data Collector End of Support Notification
items.text: Vyopta will notify customers of a new Tech Insights Data Collector release when logging in to https://my.vyopta.com

Note on the top bar there is an item labelled 'Vyopta Collector' - hovering over this will drop down a list of the collectors you are running, and display the version:

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Resolving CUCM Error: CucmMaintenanceException for listFiles

items.heading: Resolving CUCM Error: CucmMaintenanceException for listFiles
Introduction
items.text: The WXP Collaboration platform sometimes has trouble retrieving CDR records for unknown reasons. This article explains how to resolve the “CucmMaintenanceException for listFiles” error that occurs when CUCM enters a maintenance state and prevents the WXP Collaboration data collector from retrieving CDR records. For unknown reasons, a CUCM may enter a maintenance state and fail to exit this condition cleanly. When this occurs, the following message is returned when the Vyopta data collector attempts to poll for CDR records: "CucmMaintenanceException for listFiles" Whilst the CUCM is in this condition, the data collector cannot retrieve call records.

items.heading: Solution
items.text: The CUCM will need to be rebooted in order to clear this condition - as yet, no means of causing the CUCM to cleanly leave the maintenance state has been determined. In order to ascertain the cause of this issue, you may wish to raise a Cisco support case (TAC) before performing the reboot.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Explaining the Inability to View the Call Rate for CMS Acano Servers Using Tech Insights Monitoring

items.heading: Explaining the Inability to View the Call Rate for CMS Acano Servers Using Tech Insights Monitoring
Introduction
items.text: WXP Collaboration users may have difficulty viewing Call Rates related to CMS Acano servers. When viewing Call Rates for participants in Tech Insights Monitoring for a CMS/Acano conference, users only see a value of 0 for the Call Rate. Users query where they can see the Call Rate for other systems and if there is an issue.

items.heading: Solution
items.text: Unfortunately, Acano/CMS does not provide the negotiated call rate in its real-time status API. Since this is provided by most of the other infrastructure options, we have that column reserved in our presentation, but this is not an error on our side, just a limitation of Acano/CMS.

items.heading: Contact Us
items.text: For any assistance, create a support case or email support@wxp.hp.com.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Fixing WXP Collaboration CDRs Collection Issues on Linux-Based Collectors

items.heading: Fixing WXP Collaboration CDRs Collection Issues on Linux-Based Collectors
Introduction
items.text: In WXP Collaboration, when a Data Collector is on a Linux server and is restarted, CDR collection may stop working. The solution is provided below.

items.heading: Solution
items.text: The Linux Firewall rules were not saved. During reboot, the firewall reverts to the last saved settings. To fix this issue, run the following command on the Vyopta Collector Linux Server. The "save" option at the end will make the change permanent. sudo iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp -m tcp --dport 21 -j REDIRECT --to-ports 2121 sudo service iptables save For more information on installing the WXP Collaboration Collector on Linux, please refer to the Knowledge Base article.

items.heading: Contact Us
items.text: For any assistance, create a support case or email support@wxp.hp.com.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Requiring Customer Action: Cisco Webex Collection Stopping Data Collection

items.heading: Requiring Customer Action: Cisco Webex Collection Stopping Data Collection
Introduction
items.text: Restore Cisco Webex data collection in WXP Collaboration by re-authorizing OAuth credentials if your Control Hub connection has expired or been invalidated. On occasion, we have encountered issues where the oAuth authentication from Control Hub will lose or invalidate the authorization token in WXP Collaboration, which will prevent further collection of your data. When this happens, our customers will have to re-establish the oAuth connection back to Control Hub to allow the collection of data to resume.

items.heading: How Can I Fix this Issue?
items.text: In the WXP Collaboration Admin Portal, select your collection in the Infrastructure section. Click on the pencil (edit) icon to open the collection for your account.
Once you have opened the edit screen, please open the "Authorization" section and click the "Click to Authorize" button.

Once you click on the button, you will be taken to Control Hub, where you can reauthorize with an account that has full admin access to your Control Hub. Once you have re-authenticated using an admin account, your collection should resume in approximately 30 minutes (time is required for Control Hub to re-provision your API access to your admin account). If you do not see the Authorization section, that indicates you are using our Legacy PDA collection, and as a customer, you are not impacted by this breaking change. If you need further assistance, please open a support ticket, and our Support Team will be happy to help.

items.heading: Contact Us
items.text: For any assistance, create a support case or email support@wxp.hp.com.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Identifying Inconsistencies Between the Target Caller and Original Target Number

items.heading: Identifying Inconsistencies Between the Target Caller and Original Target Number
Introduction
items.text: The WXP Collaboration Platform provides visibility into call routing through the Target Caller and Original Target Number fields. This article explains how to interpret these values using the specific data from your monitored call control system.

items.heading: Question
items.text: Why are the target caller and original target number not consistent in WXP Collaboration?

items.heading: Answer
items.text: The data in the Target Caller and Original Target Number are displayed differently, according to what data is available to the WXP Collaboration system for the system monitored. The intent is always to use the 'friendly' (display name) wherever possible - this can be achieved with call control systems that provide this mapping, as, for instance, Pexip systems do. Some control systems - notably CUCM- do not provide the display name; WXP Collaboration will therefore use the alias in these fields when available. In this case, the timestamps for the last seen files should be used to determine if there has been a problem - if it is known that calls were processed since the last timestamp, then this should be investigated. WXP Collaboration has no way to identify if there is an issue with the data being sent from the call control system, so it is important to check the system logs to ensure that the data is being sent correctly.

items.text: For any assistance, create a support case or email support@wxp.hp.com.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Using your own Certificate with the WXP Collaboration Data Collector

items.heading: Using your own Certificate with the WXP Collaboration Data Collector
Introduction
items.text: The WXP Collaboration Platform supports secure data transmission through custom SSL certificate configurations. This article explains how to implement a custom certificate on your Data Collector while outlining critical limitations and support considerations for your environment.

items.heading: Using Your Certificate
items.text: From Release 4.9 of the WXP Collaboration Data Collector onwards, you have the ability to use your own certificate for endpoints and CMS to send data to the collector. All you need to do is add the following lines to your vyoptacollector.xml file. Assume the data collector's server has a FQDN of 'vyopta.customer.com' and a keystore called keystore.p12 with a keystore password of 'mypass', you would add the following properties <property key="ssl.cert.domain">vyopta.customer.com</property> <property key="ssl.cert.location">C:\Vyopta\keystore.p12</property> <property key="ssl.cert.password">mypass</property> Once you added the properties, you can restart your data collector. The next step is to open a web browser to https://your.collector.server:22181, review the certificate, and verify that it matches the one being supplied. If you are viewing your cert, you have successfully applied your custom cert to the data collector.

NoteThis is an advanced setting in WXP Collaboration, and is not supported or recommended. If you choose to provide your own certificate to the data collector, you will need to self-support any potential issues related to TLS and SNI. The example below shows that the HTTP feedback port on an endpoint cannot connect to the data collector because of SNI enforcement in the certificate. Note This is an advanced setting in WXP Collaboration, and is not supported or recommended. If you choose to provide your own certificate to the data collector, you will need to self-support any potential issues related to TLS and SNI. The example below shows that the HTTP feedback port on an endpoint cannot connect to the data collector because of SNI enforcement in the certificate.
If you run into issues like the ones above, our only recommendation is to use the default certificate provided in the WXP Collaboration Data Collector rather than a custom certificate.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Setting Up freeFTPd for WXP Collaboration Monitoring

items.heading: Setting Up freeFTPd for WXP Collaboration Monitoring
Introduction
items.text: The WXP Collaboration Platform utilizes freeFTPd to facilitate secure file transfers for monitoring activities. This article explains how to install, configure, and test your freeFTPd environment, while providing troubleshooting steps to resolve common connection issues.

items.heading: Obtaining FreeFTPd
items.text: freeFTPd is an open-source FTP/SFTP server for Windows that can be downloaded from http://www.freesshd.com/freeFTPd.exe without registration.

items.heading: Installing the Server
items.text: Once downloaded, the executable may be simply double-clicked to install the server. If UAC is triggered, then select Yes to allow the installation to proceed. Step through to accept the defaults at each stage of installation, and click Close when prompted to try other products. The final step of the installation will ask if you wish to run the program as a system service.
Select Yes, and once the service has started, click Finish.

items.heading: Configuring the Server
items.text: The freeFTPd utility should be started using the desktop shortcut or from Start. NoteThis utility should be executed with Run as administrator Note This utility should be executed with Run as administrator Once running, the utility will allow the server to be configured, and its initial status will be displayed.
Change the SFTP root directory to C:\WXPCollaboration\SFTP and select Start to run the server. If prompted to permit network access, select Allow access.
Finally, in this pane, click Apply and Save.

Specify Login as VyoptaSFTP and ensure that Authorization is set to Password saved as a SHA1 hash by selecting the appropriate item from the dropdown. Choose and specify a password as appropriate, and finally ensure that the Home directory is specified as $SERVERROOT - this will need to be changed from the default value. Unselect the FTP server, leaving only SFTP enabled. Click Apply A panel will now display a summary of the configured user(s). Click Apply & Save.
As soon as is practicable, you should reboot the server and verify that the service started as expected.

items.heading: Post Installation Testing
items.text: ● Verify that you are able to connect to localhost using the expected credentials. ○ Install WinSCP on the machine running the data collector. ○ Connect to localhost. ○ Enter the username and password configured previously. ● Using the session established ○ Transfer any file. ○ Verify that the file is present in the expected directory and delete it. ● Perform the above tests from another machine ○ Verify that you can connect and transfer a file, as above. ● If possible, perform the above tests from a machine on the same network segment as your CUCM. ○ This will verify that connectivity is permitted over the same path as will be used in production.

items.heading: Troubleshooting
items.text: ● The service has not started. ○ Verify that the service is set to start automatically (run services.msc and locate the service, right-click to obtain its properties, and select the appropriate startup type). ○ Verify that no other service is using port 22 (start a command prompt as administrator and run netstat -ban | more – find the entry for 0.0.0.0:22 and look at the name of the program beneath it).

items.text: For any assistance, create a support case or email support@wxp.hp.com.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Resolving Expired Certificate Alerts on CUBE Devices Identified by WXP Collaboration

items.heading: Resolving Expired Certificate Alerts on CUBE Devices Identified by WXP Collaboration
Introduction
items.text: This article explains why the WXP Collaboration Daily System Report flags an expired certificate on a CUBE device and outlines the steps and impact of updating the certificate to restore secure data collection.

items.heading: Problem Description:
items.text: The WXP Collaboration Daily System Report has identified that I have an expired certificate on a CUBE device. WXP Collaboration collects data from a CUBE via multiple mechanisms: the primary data path is via SNMP, but there is a secondary data path that utilises a TLS connection, which is secured by a certificate installed on the CUBE that has expired.

items.heading: Impact
items.text: Whilst this impacts the secondary collection path, SNMP is delivered over UDP, which may be less reliable in some network environments. It is therefore important to ensure that the secondary path is maintained as a confirmatory mechanism for your data.

items.heading: Resolution
items.text: For information on updating this certificate, please consult the relevant Cisco documentation or raise a TAC request with Cisco if assistance is required.

items.text: For any assistance, create a support case or email support@wxp.hp.com.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Resolving Error Indicating Data Collector Is Already Running During Installation of Data Collector

items.heading: Resolving Error Indicating Data Collector Is Already Running During Installation of Data Collector
Introduction
items.text: When installing the WXP Collaboration Data Collector, you may encounter an error indicating that another instance of the Data Collector is already running on the server. This typically occurs when an existing service using Non-Sucking Service Manager (NSSM) is already active.To resolve this issue, you must identify and stop the conflicting service before proceeding with the installation.

items.heading: Identifying NSSM Services
items.text: 1. Open PowerShell on the server as an Administrator.2. Run the following command: Get-WmiObject win32_service | ?{$_.PathName -like '*nssm*'} | select Name, DisplayName, State, PathName You should see only one service as shown the screenshot below.

items.heading: Resolving Conflicting Services
items.text: If additional services appear in the list, stop the extra service that is using NSSM. If the service cannot be stopped because other applications depend on it, install the Data Collector on a different server that does not have NSSM-dependent applications.This is a known limitation and support for coexistence with other NSSM-based applications will be addressed in a future release.

items.heading: Deleting the Existing Data Collector Service
items.text: Run the following command from an Administrator Command Prompt: SC DELETE VyoptaTech InsightsDataCollectorService

items.heading: Recreating the Data Collector Service
items.text: 1. Go to the Data Collector installation folder.2. From an Administrator Command Prompt, run: vyoptacollector.exe -install After the command completes, the new service will be created and available to start.

items.heading: Additional Resources
items.text: To uninstall the Tech Insights Data Collector service, see the article - Upgrading the WXP Collaboration Data Collector.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Resolving Login or Browser Issues When Connecting to WXP Collaboration

items.heading: Resolving Login or Browser Issues When Connecting to WXP Collaboration
items.text: When attempting to log in to the HP WXP Collaboration platform, you may encounter unexpected errors, such as missing dashboard links, error messages, or sessions timing out shortly after login. In most cases, these issues occur after a user interface upgrade in the WXP Collaboration, while your local browser is still using an older cached version of the web pages. This cached content may be incompatible with the updated web pages.

items.heading: Clearing Browser Cache
items.text: Clearing the browser cache removes locally stored website content and forces the browser to download the most recent version of all web pages.

items.heading: Using a Private Browser Session
items.text: A private browser session does not retain browsing history, cookies, site data, or form information. This can help determine whether cached data is causing login issues.

items.heading: Mozilla Firefox (Private Window)
items.text: 1. Click the menu option at the top-right corner of the browser.2. Select New Private Window.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Resolving Email Delivery Issues with Alerts and Reports

items.heading: Resolving Email Delivery Issues with Alerts and Reports
items.text: In some cases, users may notice that scheduled reports or alert notification emails are not received from HP WXP Collaboration application. This can occur even though the reports or alerts are configured correctly in the platform. Email delivery issues can occur when the email service provider detects repeated delivery failures to one or more recipient addresses within your organisation. These failures may be caused by invalid email addresses, former employees (leavers), inactive mailboxes, or incorrect distribution lists.

items.heading: Recommended Actions
items.text: To help resolve this issue, review and maintain the list of email recipients configured for alerts and reports: Maintaining accurate and up-to-date recipient email lists for alerts and reports is essential. Email delivery failures caused by recipient-related issues, such as invalid or unreachable addresses, are outside the control of WXP Collaboration.

items.heading: Contact Us
items.text: For any assistance, create a support case or email support@wxp.hp.com.

items.heading: Setting CdrEnabled and CdrLogCallsWithZeroDurationFlag Cluster-Wide
items.text: During CUCM validation in HP WXP Collaboration application, you may see errors stating that the CdrEnabled and CdrLogCallsWithZeroDurationFlag parameters are not set to True on some cluster nodes. These errors can appear even if those nodes do not handle CDRs. This article explains the reason to enable CdrEnabled and CdrLogCallsWithZeroDurationFlag settings on all nodes in a Cisco Unified Communications Manager (CUCM) cluster. When these configuration settings are queried through the CUCM API, the cluster returns a response for each node in the cluster. The API response does not identify the functional role of each node, such as whether it performs call processing, and thus generate CDRs. Because the node roles are not distinguished in the API response, the validation process cannot selectively exclude non–call-processing nodes. As a result, the settings must be enabled on all nodes in the cluster. Applying these settings cluster-wide ensures consistent behavior and prevents validation errors or other configuration issues from being hidden or misinterpreted.

items.heading: Contact Us
items.text: For any assistance, create a support case or email support@wxp.hp.com.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Unable to see Call Rate for CMS Acano servers using Tech Insights Monitoring

items.heading: Unable to see Call Rate for CMS Acano servers using Tech Insights Monitoring
items.text: When viewing participant Call Rates for a CMS/Acano conference in Tech Insights Monitoring of HP WXP Collaboration application, the Call Rate value may appear as 0. This occurs even though Call Rate values are displayed correctly for other conferencing systems.

items.heading: Solution
items.text: Cisco Meeting Server (Acano/CMS) does not provide the negotiated call rate through its real-time status API. Because most other supported infrastructure platforms provide this information, the Call Rate column is available in the Tech Insights Monitoring interface. This behavior is not an error in Tech Insights Monitoring. It is a limitation of the Acano/CMS platform and the data exposed through its API.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Data Collector Stops Collecting CDRs on Linux Based Collectors

items.heading: Data Collector Stops Collecting CDRs on Linux Based Collectors
Introduction
items.text: When a HP WXP Collaboration Data Collector running on a Linux server is restarted, the Call Detail Record (CDR) collection may stop. This issue occurs because the Linux firewall rules are not saved. After a system reboot, the firewall reverts to the last saved configuration, which may block CDR collection.

items.heading: Resolution
items.text: To resolve this issue, save the required firewall rules so they persist after a reboot. On the Linux server hosting the Data Collector, run the following commands. The save option ensures that the firewall configuration is retained permanently. sudo iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp -m tcp --dport 21 -j REDIRECT --to-ports 2121 sudo service iptables save

items.heading: Additional Resources
items.text: For information about installing and configuring the WXP Collaboration Data Collector on Linux, refer to this article - Installing WXP Collaboration Data Collector

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: Authenticating to Use the Query Service API

items.heading: Authenticating to Use the Query Service API
items.text: The credentials used to authenticate against the Assurance API are the same as your normal WXP Collaboration user credentials.

items.heading: Obtaining Authorization:
items.text: The API authentication is performed via OpenID, with the credentials being sent as an application/x-www-form-url-encoded payload to a POST request to https://login.wxpcollaboration.hp.com/auth/login. The Content-Type header must be set, and a header with the key of Host must also be set - this header takes a value of login.wxpcollaboration.hp.com.
The encoded payload contains the following key-value pairs:

Upon successful authentication, a 200 response will be received with a JSON payload with the following schema: { "access_token": string, "expires_in": int, "refresh_expires_in": int, "refresh_token": string, "token_type": string, "not_before_policy: long, "session_state": string, "scope": string } Field Description access_token The token (beginning "eyJh") that can be used to access the API expires_in The period of time, in seconds, for which this token is valid (300) refresh_expires_in The period of valid time for the refresh_token. NOT IMPLEMENTED refresh_token A token to refresh the authentication. NOT IMPLEMENTED token_type The authentication token type. Will have the value of "bearer" not_before_policy The start of signing key validity for the access_token session_state Session information scope The basis upon which the token was authenticated. Will have the value of "email profile" An example response (abbreviated) can be seen here: { "access_token":"eyJhb ... -Pdl31W5g", "expires_in":300, "refresh_expires_in":1800, "refresh_token":"ey J...-H5oAwI8", "token_type":"bearer", "not-before-policy":1533920635, "session_state":"dad914da-2d68-4202-a98a-146da4033e1e", "scope":"emailprofile" }

items.heading: Using the Access Token:
items.text: To use the token, add an HTTP header with key Authorization and a value comprised of the word Bearer, a single space, and then the token.
It is worth noting that it is the developer's responsibility to ensure that the token is within its period of validity.

https://learn.workforceexperience.hp.com.title: The Tech Insights Monitoring Query API - Information Queries

items.heading: The Tech Insights Monitoring Query API - Information Queries
wxpIntroduction
items.text: This document aims to show the usage of the Tech Insights Monitoring Query API when using the queries related to calls, endpoints, systems, meetings and trunks. These calls are illustrated by means of examples provided as Postman calls - it is assumed that the reader is familiar with Postman before starting. The examples comprise an integral part of this document.

items.heading: How Postman is Used
items.text: The examples are provided, attached to this article, as a Postman Collection and a Postman Environment. The purpose of the environment file is two-fold
See Using Data Variables for more information.

items.heading: Tech Insights Monitoring API Queries and Responses
Path Parameters
items.text: All request parameters are sent as URL path parameters, with the exception of the Authorization query, which is the only query to use the request body (payload).

items.heading: Query Responses
items.text: All responses are returned as JSON documents. At the head of the actual content, a header section provides information regarding result set size, pagination etc. The values returned are totalCount, totalPages, pageNumber, pageSize, and limitReached limitReached is a boolean value that indicates whether the request returned too many results to be processed as a single result-set. Should this be true then the request will need to be filtered. The other values are integers - it should be noted that page numbering is zero based. The other information is returned in a content array: { "totalCount": 500, "totalPages": 25, "pageNumber": 0, "pageSize": 20, "limitReached": true, "content": [ { "day": "2020-03-09", ... } { ... } ] }

items.heading: Query Parameters - options
items.text: Queries can be refined with options passed as URL queries. The common options are: Parameter Description start The beginning of a time window for the data end The end of the time window filter The criteria / filter for searching (optional) page The page of results (optional, defaults to 0) size The number of results per page (optional, defaults to 20) sort The field and direction for sorting (optional) - asc or desc. Multiple sorts can be performed on separate fields, each taking an order Other values are specified per-query. All relevant options are documented in the Postman query.

items.heading: Filtering
items.text: A filter, when available can be set on any value within a result set - filters may be chained with a semi-colon (;) - the resulting filter is a logical AND of the chain. The following operators can be used in filters: Operator == Equal To != Not Equal To =lt= or < Less Than =le= or ⇐ Less Than or Equal To =gt= or > Greater Than =ge= or >= Greater Than or Equal To As an example, a filter of target==BlueJeans;quality==GOOD would return the BlueJeans calls with good quality. A filter of target==Blue*;quality==GOOD Will return the same, along with any other calls matching a target beginning Blue.

items.heading: How to Use the Examples
items.text: In order to use the examples, first configure the environment with your credentials and organization ID. If you do not know your organization ID then please raise a helpdesk ticket and request that WXP Collaboration provide this. Once configured, run the Authorization query in the Postman Connection - this will set your access token as a variable in the environment. Please note that the token is valid for 300 seconds (5 minutes) only, and the Authorisation step must be repeated in order to obtain a new token. Setting a windowStart and windowEnd will allow you to perform the initial tests. These values are dates in ISO format - examples are present in the environment file. It is suggested that you run the examples that interest you - setting any values needed from previous results - for instance, running the Get Calls query to obtain the callID and startTime values needed for Get Call Details.

items.heading: The Examples and Environment
items.text: Each example contains all of the relevant query parameters, with description. The environment file contains the following values: - Variable Name Meaning vyoptaU WXP Collaboration Username (name.name@org.tld) vyoptaP WXP Collaboration password accessToken The bearer access token used to authenticate all requests. Automatically set by the Authorization query orgID The WXP Collaboration Organisation ID number corresponding to your organisation. See above. CallID The callID for a specific call returned by Get Calls callStartTime The start time of the call specified by CallID. Returned by Get Calls endpointID The endpoint ID as returned by Get Endpoints peripheralID A peripheralID as returned by Get Endpoint Details (must be prefixed with one of camera-, display-, mic-, or touchPanel-) windowStart The beginning of a time window for a query, in ISO format (e.g., 2020-02-22T23:00:00Z) windowEnd The end of a time window for a query, in ISO format (e.g., 2020-02-23T10:00:00Z) meetingID A Meeting ID as returned by Get Meetings meetingStartTime The start time of the call specified by MeetingID - returned by Get Meetings Returned results for the examples are described in Tech Insights Monitoring API - Query Responses The examples provided are as follows: - Example Name Purpose Authorization A call to the authorization API – stores the access token automatically. This token is used by all other queries. Get Calls Obtains a list of calls between the times of windowStart and windowEnd. Get Call Details Obtain the details for a specific call. Get Call Quality Details Obtain detailed information regarding call quality for this call. See Categories and Metrics specification in Postman request to tailor data returned. Get Call Records Return the call data record information for this call, or a 404 if not found. Get Endpoints Return a list of endpoints. Get Endpoints By Status The endpoint list sorted by the status field, illustrating sorting. Get Endpoint Details Return information held for a specific endpoint. Delete a Peripheral from an Endpoint Remove a specified peripheral from an endpoint. Get Systems Return a list of configured systems. Get Systems by Status The systems list sorted by the status field, illustrating the use of filtering. Get Systems With Status Not=OK The list of systems filtered to return only those that are not in OK status. Get Trunks Return a list of configured trunks. Get Meetings Obtain a list of meetings between windowStart and windowEnd. Get Meeting Participants Return the list of participants for a specific meeting. VyoptaAPI.postman_environment.json CPMM_API.postman_collection.json

items.heading: Contact Us
items.text: For any assistance, create a support case or email support@wxp.hp.com.

items.heading: Tech_Insights Monitoring API - Query Responses
items.text: The Query API always returns information as a JSON payload, of one of two forms. When the data returned can only be a single object, then exactly that JSON object is returned. When the data contains multiple objects, then the return is a header { "totalCount": <number>, "totalPages": <number>, "pageNumber": <number>, "pageSize": <number>, "limitReached": <boolean>, "content": [ { ... }, ... ]} The common header section was discussed in The Tech Insights Monitoring Query API - Information Queries - this document contains details of the content array or JSON object for each REST query type. items.heading: Introduction
items.text: In contrast to the Tech Insights/M API queries, all metrics are obtained by POSTing the request to a single endpoint. The nature of the metric being requested is determined by parameters sent to https://api.vyopta.com/cpmm/api/v1/orgs/{orgId}/metrics In further contrast, all parameters are sent in a JSON request body, with content type of application/json. There are two types of metrics available - KPI, which returns a single value, and trend, which returns time-series data downsampled appropriately for the duration requested. Metrics calls are authenticated in the same manner as all other API calls. Please see How Do I Authenticate to use the Query Service API? for more information, or consult the Postman examples.

items.heading: Prerequisites
items.text: Refer to the following articles: Endpoint POST https://api.vyopta.com/cpmm/api/v1/orgs/{orgId}/metrics Request body format There are two types of metrics:

items.heading: Device filters (models)
items.text: Filter metrics by device type. Get the list of valid device types with: GET https://api.vyopta.com/cpmm/api/v1/orgs/{orgId}/metrics/models The response is a JSON array. (See the Postman example "Names" in the Metrics folder.)

items.heading: Available metrics (names)
items.text: Get the list of supported metric names with: GET https://api.vyopta.com/cpmm/api/v1/orgs/{orgId}/metrics/names

items.heading: Metric types
items.text: KPI Trend

items.heading: Refining the request
items.text: Filtering Requests can contain a filter in the query parameter of the JSON body. Filters are constructed in the same way as for the API query calls, although care to correctly escape quotation characters etc (using a back-slash) should be taken, as per JSON syntax requirements. The items available for filtering differ by device types. Endpoints System devices Example "query": "tags==\"system_id=1*\" or tags==\"system_id=3\" or tags==\"type=RMX\"" Grouping Group results with the group_by field. Provide a comma-separated list of fields such as: "group_by": "type" Aggregation The returned values will be determined by the aggregation function used, such as: Downsampling The data returned as time-series data may be down-sampled depending upon the time window selected. The range of down-sampling is from 10 minutes to 12 hours (10, 60, or 720 minutes). Should higher precision data be required then the query window should be reduced.

items.heading: Tech Insights Monitoring API - Getting Started
Introduction
items.text: The Tech Insights Monitoring API gives you access to real-time and near real-time data from your organization, including: Your existing Tech Insights Dial Back feature is also available through the API, based on the dial-back package in your account.

items.heading: Intended use
items.text: The goal of this API is to let external applications consume WXP collaboration’s real-time and near real-time data as we collect it from your environment.

items.heading: Out of scope
items.text: The API does not include alerting features or historical analytics data.

items.heading: Troubleshooting
items.text: If results look unexpected, compare them with Tech Insights Monitoring in the product before assuming the API data is incorrect.

items.heading: Introduction
items.text: This document is a walk-through on how to configure WXP Collaboration monitors to generate tickets in ServiceNow using webhook notifications. This document also covers the changes required in ServiceNow to enable the integration.

items.heading: Configuring Webhooks in WXP Collaboration
items.text: This section will walk through setting up a webhook for a monitor in WXP Collaboration. items.heading: Introduction
items.text: Poly Lens is an Add-on feature in HP Workforce Experience (WXP) that connects your WXP tenant to a Poly Lens Cloud tenant. IT Administrators can then deploy, monitor, update, and troubleshoot collaboration devices and rooms directly in Poly Lens Cloud. This article provides guidance to Add, Edit, and Disconnect Poly Lens Add-on feature.

items.heading: Adding Poly Lens
items.text: Follow these steps to add Poly Lens:

items.heading: Option A. Creating a New Poly Lens Account
items.text: The new Poly Lens account is created. The tenant (account) name is based on the WXP tenant name.

items.heading: Option B: Connecting an Existing Poly Lens Account
items.text: A confirmation email is sent, indicating a Poly Lens license has been added.

items.heading: Editing the Connected Poly Lens Tenant
items.text: Follow these steps update the connected Poly Lens tenant. Selecting Poly Lens from the navigation menu now opens the newly connected tenant.

items.heading: Disconnecting the Poly Lens Add-on
items.text: Follow these steps to remove the Poly Lens connection. A success message confirms the tenant was disconnected, and a confirmation email is sent. After disconnection:

items.heading: Troubleshooting Add‑on Connection Issues
items.text: Connection Error: Poly Lens connection is broken Symptom: When accessing the Poly Lens add‑on from HP Workforce Experience (WXP), the WXP IT Admin user encounters a connection failure and cannot load the linked Poly Lens tenant details. Error Message Connection Error: Poly Lens connection is broken - Disconnect and reconnect the account to fix the issue.
